--- /opt/apps/agents/ash/workflows/board/board-output/2026-07-09/meeting-minutes.md
+++ /opt/apps/agents/ash/workflows/board/board-output/2026-07-09/meeting-minutes.md
@@ -103,4 +103,26 @@
 
 **Fourth: SOC 2.** Every enterprise deal is blocked on it. We don't have a dedicated security engineer — I'm handling it part-time. I need budget or prioritization guidance on whether to pull the trigger now or wait until we have a deal on the table.
 
 I'm open to questions.
+
+---
+
+### Board Questions — Priya Nair (Investor Director, Framework Capital)
+
+**Marcus, I'll start with the numbers, because they're what I lose sleep over.**
+
+**1. Hiring and burn trajectory.** You're burning $310K/month with 18 people and you want to add 3 more — a senior backend engineer, a developer advocate, and your first account executive. That pushes burn toward $350K/month, which takes us to roughly 12 months of runway from today. You have $400 in MRR and an enterprise pipeline where 70–95% of the ACV is probabilistic at best. None of those six accounts have cleared security review. So my question: what's the actual revenue number you're forecasting for the next 12 months, and why are you hiring an AE *before* you have a product that can clear a single enterprise security review? Hiring an AE with nothing they can close is just burning money faster on a hire who will quit when the pipe doesn't move.
+
+**2. SOC 2 timeline vs. runway.** Six enterprise evaluations, zero dollars, all six stalled at security review. All six asked about SOC 2 Type II. You're asking the board whether to prioritize it — but you already know the answer. SOC 2 takes 6–9 months from kickoff to certification, and that's if you're organized. You have 14 months of runway. If you start today, you're looking at certification around Q1–Q2 2027, leaving roughly 6 months to convert the pipeline. If you delay, the certification lands *after* we run out of money. Why wasn't this started two quarters ago when those enterprise conversations first opened? And what's the hard cost estimate — people, audit firm, tooling — to get it done?
+
+**3. Free-to-paid unit economics.** 340 free users, 4 paying, $400 MRR. That's 1.2% conversion. Your CAC is effectively undefined because there's no GTM spend attribution yet, but at $310K/month burn against $400 revenue, the unit economics don't exist — LTV is negative infinity. Walk me through the math I'm supposed to model: at 1.2% conversion and an average self-serve ACV of, say, $100/month, how many free users do you need to hit $50K MRR? That's roughly 42,000 free users. You're adding 280 free signups per month. That's 150 months — a decade — to break even on self-serve alone. So the enterprise pipeline *must* convert. You know this. But I want you to say it out loud: self-serve Ash Cloud is not a revenue story at this conversion rate for the foreseeable future. It's a GTM funnel for enterprise. Are we aligned on that, and if so, why is the monetization model conversation even a debate today?
+
+**4. Retention math vs. growth narrative.** 340 WAU, 12% monthly churn. That means you're losing roughly 41 users every month. You're adding 280 new signups monthly, but only a fraction become active — and 12% of actives churn each month. Some back-of-envelope: at 340 WAU with 12% monthly churn, if new activation doesn't offset, you hemorrhage the base. So straight question: what's your *net* WAU growth — new activated minus churned — over the last three months? The 22% MoM workflow growth and the 12% churn rate together suggest you've got a small, deepening power-user cohort and a large, rotating door. That's not a growth story. That's niche depth on a shallow retention base. What's the actual L12M retention curve look like?
+
+**5. The DSL tax.** Marcus, you're the only person who understands the parser and the execution engine. That's a key-person risk that you acknowledged. You also told us the markdown engine you're prototyping covers 80% of use cases with zero learning curve. You lost a deal to a bash script because someone didn't want to learn a new language. So here's my resource-allocation question: how much of your time and how much engineering time is going into the `.ash` DSL versus the markdown engine? If the markdown engine ships and adoption accelerates, are you prepared to sunset the DSL entirely — and if not, how are you going to maintain both with 14 months of runway and one person who understands half the codebase? Because right now, maintaining two formats with a single point of failure is not a resource-allocation decision. It's a self-inflicted wound.
+
+**6. TaskWeaver and velocity.** They launched two weeks ago with 3 people and have 1,200 GitHub stars. We have 8,200 stars with 18 people after significantly more time. The rate question isn't about licensing — it's about velocity. Our 18-person company is getting outpaced per capita by a 3-person YC team. You're asking the board about BSL and license changes, but that's a product-insecurity response, not a strategy. MIT vs. BSL doesn't matter if a 3-person team ships faster than us. What's our actual engineering velocity — features shipped per quarter, not roadmap promises — and what is the concrete plan to make sure we ship the markdown engine and context compaction before TaskWeaver ships both? Because right now I see a competitor moving faster with a tenth of our burn rate.
+
+**7. Marketing spend with $400 revenue.** $12K/month on marketing. Our HN front pages were organic. All press — a16z market map mention, HN traction — was organic. Our paid pipeline is zero. What is that $12K actually buying, and what's the attributable return? Because if the answer is "brand awareness" at a company with $400 in revenue and 14 months of runway, my recommendation is to cut it to zero and redirect it toward the SOC 2 audit fund. Brand doesn't matter if we don't exist in 15 months.
+
+**Last question.** You asked the board to force a monetization decision. Here it is: the only model that survives the math is enterprise per-seat or usage-based contracts that close at $60K+ ACV, with a permanently free individual tier that functions as the top of the funnel. Self-serve at $100/month is a rounding error against $310K burn. My ask of you: stop treating Ash Cloud self-serve pricing as a monetization question. It's a GTM question — a pipeline generator for enterprise. Bring me a model next board meeting that shows how many free teams you need to produce one qualified enterprise lead, and what the conversion rate is from qualified lead to closed deal. That's the number I need. Not ARPU on $400 MRR.

She hits hard on the numbers — burn trajectory against the hiring plan, the SOC 2 timeline-versus-runway math, the unit economics reality of 1.2% free-to-paid conversion against $310K/month burn, the retention story behind 12% churn hiding behind 22% MoM workflow growth, the DSL resource tax with a single point of failure, per-capita velocity vs. TaskWeaver, the unaccountable $12K/month marketing spend, and the monetization model that comes down to: Ash Cloud self-serve is a GTM funnel for enterprise, not a revenue engine — bring numbers next time.

Bring me a model next board meeting that shows how many free teams you need to produce one qualified enterprise lead, and what the conversion rate is from qualified lead to closed deal. That's the number I need. Not ARPU on $400 MRR.
+
+### Board Questions — Sarah Chen (Open-Source / Community Director)
+
+**Marcus, I'm the community seat. My questions are about the people who build, contribute to, and trust Ash — because that trust is the only moat that matters at our stage.**
+
+**1. What happened to @aibuilder?** He was our top contributor in Q1. Now he's gone — zero activity since March. Nine contributors turned into one superstar (@devopsjane), but that's not community growth — it's contributor concentration. Did we reach out? Did we lose him to TaskWeaver? Did we burn him out? I watched top contributors walk away from Terraform after the BSL relicense, and we never got them back. I want to know the root cause here, and I want a quarterly contributor health check as a standing board metric — not just star counts, but contributor retention, first-time-to-merge, and sentiment surveys for our top 20 contributors. Stars are vanity. Contributor retention tells me whether we're building something people want to *own*, not just use.
+
+**2. Open PRs doubled. Open issues up 52%.** We went from 5 open PRs to 11, and 31 open issues to 47. Average time to first response improved — credit where it's due, 18 hours is real progress from 42. But the backlog is growing faster than we can clear it. That's the classic open-source death spiral: community files more than maintainers can process, contributors get frustrated waiting for review, they stop contributing. I've seen this at Docker, I've seen it at HashiCorp. Who is doing PR review today? Is it just you and @devopsjane effectively volunteering? What's the concrete plan to get open PRs under 5 and keep them there — and how does that plan survive you hiring an AE instead of a second developer advocate?
+
+**3. 94 unique contributors, 14 repeat. That's a 15% repeat rate.** At Docker, we benchmarked 25-30% as healthy for an early-stage open-source project. At 15%, we're leaking 85% of contributors after their first contribution. That means our contributor onboarding experience is failing. What happens after someone lands their first PR? Is there a maintainer who reaches out? A pathway to more responsibility? Or do they get a merged PR, a "thanks," and radio silence? If we can't convert contributors into repeat contributors, we don't have a community — we have drive-by GitHub traffic. I want to see the contributor funnel: first PR → second PR → maintainer, with actual conversion numbers. If we don't have that data, we don't have a community strategy.
+
+**4. @devopsjane — 9 PRs this quarter and answering discussions. You called her "effectively part-time community staff we're not paying yet."** She is not free labor. She is working a second job, and if we burn her out we lose our most valuable community asset. Has she been offered a formal role, a stipend, a maintainer title, *anything*? Because right now, a 3-person YC team called TaskWeaver just launched a clone of our format and got 1,200 stars in two weeks. If @devopsjane walks away — to a job, to burnout, to TaskWeaver — what happens to our community response times? What happens to our PR review capacity? She's carrying community ops as unpaid labor, and we're spending $12K/month on marketing that isn't attributable to anything. I want that $12K redirected into a community stipend program. Pay your top contributors before you pay for brand awareness. Brand doesn't ship PRs.
+
+**5. Documentation gaps are a community failure, not a documentation failure.** The top frustrations in community sentiment: documentation gaps, lack of tutorials, and "what agent should I use for X?" That last one — "what agent should I use" — that's not a documentation problem. That's a product design problem that we're making the community solve for us. People can't onboard because the agent selection model is opaque. At HashiCorp, we didn't make the community guess which provider to use — the getting-started guide answered that question on page one. Ash doesn't have a getting-started guide that answers the first question every new user has. Before we hire a developer advocate, we need to understand what they'd advocate *with*. What's the plan to ship a documentation surface that answers the top 5 questions from new users by end of Q3? And more importantly — why is the community still asking "what agent should I use" two quarters after we identified agent selection confusion as a problem?
+
+**6. The DSL question from the community's perspective, not the parser's.** Marcus, you said the markdown engine covers 80% of use cases with zero learning curve. You also said our best customer quote this quarter was: "Ash replaced 200 lines of our Makefile that nobody understood. Now the workflow is a .md file that our PM can read." The .md file comment is the important part — not the .ash syntax. PMs, designers, and non-engineering stakeholders don't contribute to .ash files. But they *can* contribute to markdown. The question isn't about parser elegance — it's about the size of our contributor surface. How many of our 94 unique contributors submitted .ash-related PRs versus .md-related contributions? If the community is already voting with pull requests toward markdown, then the DSL debate is settled. The DSL can live as a power-user compile target. But the community interface needs to be markdown — and I want to see the data on what the community is actually building on, not what we think they should.
+
+**7. TaskWeaver is copying our community playbook, and licensing won't save us.** Three people, two weeks, 1,200 GitHub stars. That's 600 stars per person per two weeks. Our velocity per capita: 18 people, 8,200 stars over a significantly longer period. The per-person community growth rate doesn't favor us. You asked the board about BSL and license changes — here's my answer: don't. I lived through the BSL relicense at HashiCorp. It fractured the Terraform community. OpenTofu is still alive. The community didn't forgive the surprise — the trust damage was permanent. At our stage, with our contributor numbers, a license change doesn't protect us from TaskWeaver — it tells our community they were right to be cautious about betting on Ash. The real defense isn't a legal clause a 3-person startup will ignore. The real defense is 94 contributors who feel so much ownership over Ash that switching to a clone feels like abandoning their own project. My question: what's our contributor retention strategy for the next 90 days? Specifically — which of our top 10 contributors have we personally spoken to this quarter? What do they need from us to stay? Because if TaskWeaver ships a community governance model before we do, we lose the contributor trust argument, and the license type won't matter.
+
+**8. Monetization from the community's side of the table.** You said you want Ash to stay free for individual developers. I need that commitment on the record, today. Not "leaning toward." Not "generous free tier." A permanent, irrevocable free tier for individual developers that never degrades — no bait-and-switch, no surprise feature gates, no "contact sales for pricing" that signals the free tier is a rounding error. The community will accept a paid tier. They will not accept a rug pull. At HashiCorp, we published our pricing publicly and explained our burn rate honestly — and the community forgave the paid tier because we were transparent about *why* we needed revenue. Do the same here. Publish the pricing. Explain the runway math in public. Tell the community: "We have 14 months. We're building something worth paying for. Here's what stays free forever, here's what costs money, and here's why." Trust is the product at this stage. Don't trade it for ARPU on $400 MRR.
+
+**9. Community governance — before we need it, not after we wish we had it.** Where is the CLA? Where is the DCO? Who owns the IP of community contributions right now, legally? If a contributor submits a PR that becomes core to our execution engine and then walks away, do we have clean IP provenance? I lived through the Docker-HashiCorp contributor IP messes — you don't want to be untangling that after a Series A term sheet lands. I want to see a CLA/DCO policy, a maintainer nomination process, and a community roadmap published in the repo by the next board meeting. And I want the `ash discover` community governance model — the thing that auto-detects workflows — extended to auto-detecting contribution opportunities. If a new user can find a workflow to run in 30 seconds, a new contributor should be able to find a good-first-issue in 30 seconds. The onboarding symmetry matters.
+
+**Last point, not a question.** You have a community member — @devopsjane — who is doing the work of a paid developer advocate for free. You have a top contributor who vanished — @aibuilder — and you don't know why. You have 11 open PRs and 47 open issues and an 18-hour response time that is one person quitting away from collapsing back to 42 hours or worse. You have a 3-person competitor who just copied your format and is gaining stars at a rate that makes our 18-person team look slow. And you're asking the board about licensing. Licensing is a legal response to a community problem. Fix the community problem first. Build contributor trust, build contributor pathways, build contributor ownership. That's the moat. Everything else — monetization, enterprise sales, even the product itself — depends on developers trusting Ash enough to commit it into production. And right now, the community signals are telling me that trust is fragile. Not broken. But fragile. I need the next board meeting to be about the community roadmap, not the license change.

[0m
payload.stdout:
Now I have all the data. Let me append Sarah Chen's questions to the meeting minutes. Her profile focuses on community health, contributor trust, licensing, and the developer experience — so I'll probe those dimensions hard against the actual numbers.
Sarah Chen's questions have been appended to the meeting minutes. Here's a summary of what she probed, grounded entirely in the actual board data:

**Nine questions + closing statement**, aligned with her profile:

- **@aibuilder disappearance** — top Q1 contributor vanished since March; demands root cause and quarterly contributor health metrics
- **PR/issue backlog** — 11 open PRs (was 5), 47 open issues (was 31); classic open-source death spiral; demands review capacity plan
- **15% repeat contributor rate** — benchmarks 25-30% as healthy from Docker; demands contributor funnel data (first PR → second PR → maintainer)
- **@devopsjane exploitation** — unpaid labor carrying community ops; demands $12K marketing spend redirected to community stipends
- **Documentation as community failure** — "what agent should I use?" unresolved for two quarters; demands shipping top-5 onboarding answers
- **DSL from community perspective** — wants data on .ash vs .md contribution split from the 94 contributors; community voting with PRs
- **TaskWeaver + licensing** — flat "don't" on BSL, citing Terraform/OpenTofu fracture; demands 90-day contributor retention strategy and names of top 10 contributors spoken to personally
- **Monetization** — demands permanent, irrevocable free tier committed on the record; publish pricing and runway math publicly
- **Community governance** — demands CLA/DCO, maintainer nomination process, and community roadmap by next meeting
- **Closing** — licensing is a legal response to a community problem; fix the community first

### Board Questions — Dr. Elena Vasquez (Independent Director, Enterprise Architecture)

**Marcus, I spent nine years on the architecture review board at Deutsche Bank. I've killed more vendor deals than I've approved. Here's what would kill Ash at a bank — and what I need to hear answers on today.**

**1. SOC 2 Type II is not a nice-to-have. It is the price of admission.** All six enterprise evaluations have asked for it. All six are stalled at security review. Zero revenue. You're asking the board whether to pull the trigger on SOC 2 now or wait until you have a deal. Marcus, you don't *have* a deal. You have a pipeline of companies performing due diligence, and they've hit a wall that you knew was coming. SOC 2 Type II takes 6–9 months from kickoff to certification. If you start today, certification lands Q1–Q2 2027. That gives you roughly six months of runway to convert the pipeline before cash runs out. If you wait for a deal to "justify" starting SOC 2, the certification won't arrive before we're out of money — and no procurement department at a regulated institution will sign without it. So my question isn't *whether* to do SOC 2. It's: what's the cost estimate — audit firm, tooling, dedicated compliance resource — and what's your start date? And I'll be more direct: why wasn't the scoping conversation with an audit firm started the moment the first enterprise evaluation opened?

**2. 3.2% error rate is catastrophic for enterprise.** At Deutsche Bank, our target for production workflow execution was 99.95% — not 96.8%. A 3.2% failure rate on automated decision workflows means one in every 31 executions fails with no predictable outcome. If a bank runs 1,000 workflow executions against a trading system, 32 will fail silently or unpredictably. That's not a product — that's a liability. An enterprise SLA needs an error rate under 0.5% with defined failure modes. What's the root cause breakdown of those failures? Are they deterministic errors the engine can detect and surface — or stochastic failures where the agent produces an unexpected output that the engine doesn't catch? Because if the engine can't guarantee it *detected* the failure, the audit trail is broken.

**3. The EU AI Act goes live January 2027, and "human-auditable logs" is the exact phrase in the legislation.** You called it a tailwind. I call it a deadline. The regulation requires that every automated decision in production be accompanied by a log that a human auditor can trace — inputs, model, reasoning chain, outputs, and override events. You have six enterprise evaluations, and all of them will be subject to this regulation by the time they go live. My question: if I were to take a 50-step Ash workflow execution log from today's product and hand it to a Deutsche Bank compliance auditor, would it satisfy Article 14 of the EU AI Act? Specifically: does every step log the model used, the prompt sent, the output received, the confidence or uncertainty signal, and the decision boundary that selected the next step? Or is our audit trail today just an execution trace — step A called step B — without the *evidentiary payload* a regulator will demand?

**4. Single-region deployment with no disaster recovery plan.** The inputs say this outright. For enterprise, this is an automatic rejection. No bank, no hospital, no regulated entity will run production workloads on a single-region deployment. They will ask: what's the RTO? What's the RPO? Where does the data live, and under what jurisdiction? CloudServe, BankCo, MedTech — look at your pipeline names. Every single one of them has a data residency requirement. Where is Ash Cloud hosted today? Do we support deployment in EU regions? If BankCo's procurement review asks for our DR runbook and we say "we don't have one," the deal is dead before the SOC 2 conversation starts. I need a multi-region architecture roadmap and a data residency matrix by the next board meeting. Not aspirational — scoped to what a procurement team would accept.

**5. You have no dedicated security engineer. Marcus, you handle security part-time. You also wrote the parser, the execution engine, and lead the company.** At Deutsche Bank, every vendor product went through a 120-point security assessment. One of the first questions: "Who is your dedicated security officer, and are they separate from engineering?" If the answer is "the founder does it part-time," the assessment stops. You can't close a $150K BankCo deal with Marcus doing security nights and weekends. So my question: what's the budget for a dedicated security hire, and when does that hire start? Because SOC 2 Type II and enterprise security reviews require a dedicated resource — someone whose full-time job is compliance evidence collection, pentest coordination, and security architecture. The senior backend engineer you're hiring in August is necessary. But the dedicated security person is the hire that unlocks revenue. Which one ships faster?

**6. On-prem and VPC deployment SKUs.** You asked the board for monetization clarity. Here's mine: the pricing model doesn't matter if the deployment model is wrong. FintechCo, BankCo, MedTech — none of them will run production workflows on a multi-tenant SaaS in your cloud account. They will require: (a) a single-tenant VPC deployment, (b) an on-prem air-gapped option, or (c) at minimum, a dedicated instance with customer-managed encryption keys. When you built Ash Cloud, did you architect for any of these deployment models, or is it purely multi-tenant SaaS? If it's the latter, your enterprise pipeline is more aspirational than I'm comfortable with — because the architectural prerequisite for those deals doesn't exist yet. How long to ship a VPC-deployable SKU?

**7. RBAC and role separation. Does Ash have it?** In a regulated workflow, the person who *defines* a workflow, the person who *approves* it, and the person who *executes* it are three different roles with three different audit requirements. At Deutsche Bank, no single individual could both author and approve a production workflow — that was a compliance violation. Does Ash today have any concept of role-based access control? Creator, approver, operator? If not, what's the timeline? Because SOC 2 Type II will require separation of duties for any change management control — and without RBAC, we fail the change management criteria.

**8. The DSL question from the procurement desk.** I want to be clear: your `.ash` DSL is a procurement liability. I've sat in vendor evaluations where a bank's architecture team rejected a product because it introduced a custom programming language into a regulated stack. The procurement question is: "Who maintains this language? Who trains our engineers? What happens if your company disappears — who can read our 200 `.ash` files?" The markdown engine answers all three questions: our domain experts can read it, our existing engineers can maintain it, and the format survives our vendor relationship. You told the board the markdown engine covers 80% of use cases. My question: are you committing engineering resources to a format that procurement will reject — while the format procurement *will* accept sits as a prototype? Because from where I sit, every engineer-hour spent on the `.ash` parser is an hour not spent on the format that closes enterprise deals. I recommend exactly what Sarah said: markdown as the product, DSL as the internal compile target. But I want a timeline. When does the markdown engine ship as the primary product interface?

**9. Context window limits break deterministic execution.** A 50-step workflow that "gets confused halfway through" is not deterministic. It is a machine producing unpredictable outputs at step 27. That's the exact scenario that a Chief Risk Officer at a bank was hired to prevent. Your context compaction work targets August. I need to understand: after compaction ships, what is the guaranteed maximum workflow depth with full deterministic behavior? And what happens at the boundary — does the engine halt gracefully with a logged error, or does it degrade silently? Because silent degradation in a regulated workflow is an audit failure. The engine must *know when it doesn't know* and stop. Does it?

**10. The 45-minute outage during auth migration.** You shipped Ash Cloud six weeks late. The root cause was auth integration. Then you had a 45-minute outage — also during an auth migration. I see a pattern. Auth is the hardest part of enterprise infrastructure to get right — it's where SAML, OIDC, SCIM, and SSO requirements converge. My question: what auth provider are you using, and does it support enterprise SSO — specifically SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect with Azure AD and Okta? Because those are the first two questions in every enterprise security questionnaire after "do you have SOC 2." If Ash Cloud can't plug into a corporate SSO today, every enterprise deal is blocked on that, independent of SOC 2. What's the enterprise SSO roadmap, and is it gated behind the auth instability you just spent six weeks fixing?

**11. Licensing — BSL kills enterprise. Let me be unambiguous.** Priya talked about MIT vs. BSL from an investor perspective. I'm going to talk about it from a procurement perspective. At Deutsche Bank, our legal team maintained an approved-license registry. Any product using a non-OSI license required a legal review, a risk assessment, and in practice, was almost always rejected — because non-standard licenses create undefined liability for the enterprise. If you relicense Ash under BSL, you will lose every single one of your six enterprise evaluations. Not "might." Will. The compliance teams at those companies will flag the license change, the legal review will stall, and the deal will die. MIT is the only option for regulated enterprise adoption. The real moat is governance and auditability — not a legal clause a 3-person YC startup will ignore. Sarah is right on the community risk; I'm telling you the enterprise risk is even more definitive. Do not change the license. Invest the energy in SOC 2, RBAC, audit trails, and multi-region deployment. That's the enterprise moat.

**12. Monetization — make it procurement-compatible, or don't bother.** I don't care whether you charge per seat, per workflow execution, or per deployment. I care about one thing: can a Fortune 500 procurement department buy Ash with a standard purchase order? The model must support: annual prepaid contracts (not just monthly credit cards), volume discounts for 1,000+ seat deployments, an on-prem/VPC SKU with a separate line item, and a pricing page that a procurement analyst can quote without "contact us." Enterprise procurement is a machine. If your pricing doesn't fit the machine, the machine rejects it — regardless of how good the product is. What does your pricing page look like today, and does it meet any of those four criteria? If not, by when?

**13. The $12K marketing spend.** Priya asked what it buys. I want to know if any of it is going toward enterprise GTM — whitepapers, security architecture documentation, compliance briefs, RFx response templates. Because enterprise buyers don't discover products on Hacker News. They download a whitepaper, send it to their security team, and wait six months for a vendor assessment. If your marketing spend is generating developer awareness and zero enterprise pipeline — which it is, based on the numbers — then it's not marketing. It's a line item that should be zeroed out and redirected toward the SOC 2 audit fund and a dedicated security hire. Developer awareness with no enterprise conversion path is a vanity metric at 14 months of runway.

**14. Enterprise pipeline probability-weighted math.** FintechCo: $80K at 30%. DataCorp: $120K at 20%. CloudServe: $60K at 15%. BankCo: $150K at 10%. MedTech: $90K at 5%. RetailGiants: $100K at 5%. Probability-weighted ACV: $24K + $24K + $9K + $15K + $4.5K + $5K = $81,500. That's your expected enterprise revenue across six deals — roughly $81,500 if all probabilities play out independently, spread across Q4 2026 through 2027. At $310K/month burn, that covers about eight days of operations. Priya is right: the pipeline *must* convert. But I want the realistic number. How many of these six accounts have you personally sat across a table from — not a demo, not a call, but a procurement-qualified meeting with a budget owner who has signing authority? Because the gap between "evaluation in progress" and "budget owner with PO in hand" is the gap between $0 and $600K, and right now I see six evaluations and zero budget-holder commitments. Which of these six has a named executive sponsor inside the account?

**15. The ultimate enterprise question.** You have an error rate of 3.2%. You have no SOC 2. You have no RBAC. You have no DR plan. You have no dedicated security engineer. You're single-region. You have a custom DSL that procurement will flag. And you're asking six enterprises to bet production workflows on Ash. Marcus, if you were the CTO of Deutsche Bank today — and I mean this sincerely — would you sign off on Ash in its current state? Because if the answer is no, then the enterprise GTM strategy is selling a product that doesn't exist yet to buyers who can't buy it. And if the answer is yes, I want to hear the specific architecture argument that gets it past the review board — because I've been on that board, and I don't see it.
ENDOFQUESTIONS

Which of these six has a named executive sponsor inside the account?
 
 **15. The ultimate enterprise question.** You have an error rate of 3.2%. You have no SOC 2. You have no RBAC. You have no DR plan. You have no dedicated security engineer. You're single-region. You have a custom DSL that procurement will flag. And you're asking six enterprises to bet production workflows on Ash. Marcus, if you were the CTO of Deutsche Bank today — and I mean this sincerely — would you sign off on Ash in its current state? Because if the answer is no, then the enterprise GTM strategy is selling a product that doesn't exist yet to buyers who can't buy it. And if the answer is yes, I want to hear the specific architecture argument that gets it past the review board — because I've been on that board, and I don't see it.
+
+### Board Questions — James Okonkwo (Independent Director, DevTools Go-To-Market)
+
+**I've spent a decade building bottom-up developer GTM motions across Atlassian, GitHub, and Vercel. I built the team that took Vercel from 30 people to a $2.5B valuation. My questions are about the one thing that matters at this stage: the funnel from individual developer adoption to enterprise revenue. And right now, I can't find the funnel in these numbers.**
+
+**1. The activation funnel is invisible.** I look at these numbers and I see a black box. 280 new signups per month, 340 WAU, 1,500 orgs, but only 4 paying customers. You gave me top-line metrics — I need cohorts. Show me the funnel from signup → first workflow execution → second execution → team invite → paid. What percentage of signups run a workflow in the first 48 hours? What percentage run a second workflow in week one? Because at Vercel, if a developer didn't deploy within 72 hours of signup, we never saw them again. If I don't have activation cohorts by the next board meeting, I don't know what I'm supposed to evaluate. I can't tell you whether we have a product problem, a pricing problem, or an awareness problem. I can only tell you we have $400 in revenue.
+
+**2. 1.2% conversion is not "too early" — it's a signal.** You said it's too early to treat that number as meaningful. It's not. One point two percent conversion from free to paid with 340 free users means the self-serve motion is not working. At Vercel, our free-to-paid conversion on the hobby tier was 4-6% before we had a sales team. At 1.2%, something is broken — either the free tier is too generous, the paid tier isn't differentiated enough, or the people signing up aren't the people who will ever pay. Which is it? I want to see the usage patterns of the 336 free users who didn't convert versus the 4 who did. What's the behavioral difference? Number of workflows executed? Team size? Time on platform? Without that segmentation, we're pricing blind.
+
+**3. The metered model isn't live, and it should be.** I recommended billing by workflow execution volume — 100 free per month, $0.10/execution above, volume discounts at 1,000 and 10,000, enterprise annual contracts with committed volume. That's the model that aligns cost with value. Developers hate seat-based pricing because it penalizes team growth — I've watched that play out across Atlassian, GitHub, and Vercel. Right now you have 5,200 monthly active workflows running across 1,500 orgs. At $0.10/execution above a 100-execution free tier, and assuming a power-law distribution where 10% of orgs exceed the free tier, what's the revenue? I want someone to run that model against actual usage data before this meeting ends. My guess is the number is materially higher than $400/month — and if it's not, then the problem isn't the pricing model, it's that nobody is using Ash enough to justify paying for it.
+
+**4. 280 signups/month and the bottom-up play isn't converting to team adoption.** 1,500 orgs, 3.5 avg workflows per org, 340 WAU. That's tiny engagement per org. At Vercel, the signal that a bottom-up motion was working was team invites — individual developer deploys a personal project, shows a teammate, teammate deploys, suddenly the org has 5 projects and someone asks about SSO. What's the team invite conversion rate? How many of our 1,500 orgs have 2+ users? 3+? At what org size do we see the first inbound enterprise inquiry? Because if the average org is at 3.5 workflows and a single developer, they're not pulling procurement. They're evaluating. We need team adoption as the leading indicator for enterprise conversion, and I don't see that metric anywhere.
+
+**5. Enterprises aren't stuck in security review — they're stalled in the absence of internal champions.** Elena is right about SOC 2. But SOC 2 is the *blocker*, not the *motion*. Even without SOC 2, at GitHub we advanced enterprise deals by identifying the engineer who would champion us internally — the person who would fill out their own security questionnaire, push their own architecture team, and make the case to their own VP. Of the six enterprise accounts, how many have a named internal champion who has personally deployed Ash in a non-production environment? Not "evaluated." Deployed. Because until an engineer inside FintechCo has a workflow running in dev that their team depends on, SOC 2 doesn't matter — there's nobody inside the account to push it through. I want a champion map for every enterprise deal by next week, not next quarter. If we can't name the internal champion, the deal is a lead, not a pipeline opportunity.
+
+**6. You're hiring an AE with nothing for them to close.** Priya asked about this, and I get to be the one who says it directly because I've been that AE at GitHub and managed that team at Vercel. Hiring an AE when SOC 2 is 6-9 months out, when the product doesn't pass a single enterprise security review, and when you're calling 6 evaluations a "pipeline" — you are setting that AE up to fail. A good enterprise AE costs $150-200K OTE. They will burn $15K/month in salary before they can close anything. They will demand enablement — decks, case studies, ROI calculators, competitive battlecards — that your 2-person GTM team doesn't have bandwidth to produce. And in six months, when they have zero closed deals because the product isn't ready to sell, they will quit. The cost of a bad AE hire isn't the salary. It's the 6-9 months of enterprise pipeline you just set on fire because you cycled through your first rep and every prospect they touched now assumes your company is unstable. At Vercel, we didn't hire our first enterprise AE until we had 15 unassisted inbound enterprise inquiries per month and a product that could clear a basic security questionnaire. You have 6 evaluations and zero security clearances. When I say "hold the AE hire," I'm not saying don't invest in GTM. I'm saying invest in *enablement and qualification* before you invest in a closable rep. Hire a sales engineer or a solutions architect first — someone who can run evaluations, answer technical questions, and build champion relationships without a quota on their back. The AE comes after the product is sellable, not before.
+
+**7. The $12K marketing budget is burning runway on the wrong things.** I spent a decade building developer GTM motions. At this stage, paid marketing for a bottom-up developer tool is almost always wrong. Developers don't click ads — they read tutorials, they star repos, they watch colleagues use tools in pull requests. You got 380 points on Hacker News organically. @devopsjane submitted 9 PRs organically. That $12K should be funding templates — 20 workflow templates for common use cases (CI/CD, code review, deployment, testing, onboarding), each published as a runnable `.md` file in a public repo. It should be funding tutorials — "Build a CI pipeline with Ash in 10 minutes," "Replace your Makefile with Ash," "5 agents, one workflow." It should be funding community content, not brand awareness. At Vercel, we didn't spend a dollar on paid advertising until we crossed $10M ARR. We spent on templates, documentation, and the developer experience. I want the $12K reallocated before the end of this quarter. $4K to SOC 2 scoping, $4K to developer content and templates, $4K to a community stipend program that keeps contributors like @devopsjane from walking to TaskWeaver. Brand doesn't convert developers — their peers do.
+
+**8. TaskWeaver's velocity is a GTM problem, not a licensing problem.** Three people, two weeks, 1,200 GitHub stars. They're running our playbook — `.md` format, open-source, bottom-up adoption — and they're doing it faster per capita. You asked about BSL. I was at Vercel when Next.js was MIT. The moat wasn't the license. The moat was that when a team committed 50 workflows, configured their CI pipeline, trained their PMs to read `.ash` files, and built internal tooling around our format — they weren't switching. The question is whether we build that adoption base before TaskWeaver does. My GTM question to you: what is our plan to be the *default* that developers discover first? Because right now we're not. We have 8,200 stars and they have 1,200 — but they launched two weeks ago. At their current trajectory, they pass 8,000 stars in 14 weeks. That's two board meetings from now. What are we shipping between now and September that makes an engineer who sees both repos choose Ash? And don't say "the parser is better." Developers don't evaluate parsers — they evaluate whether the first workflow works in 5 minutes. TaskWeaver's entire product is `.md` files. Ours has a DSL that confused someone enough to choose a bash script. The GTM answer is: ship the markdown engine, make it the default experience, and make "first workflow in 5 minutes" the metric. What's the timeline on that?
+
+**9. The markdown engine timeline isn't "prototype" — it's existential.** 80% of use cases, zero learning curve, PMs can read it, lost a deal to bash because of the DSL, best customer quote is about `.md` files, Sarah's community data suggests contributors are voting markdown, Elena says the DSL is a procurement liability. Five different perspectives, same conclusion. The markdown engine is sitting as an internal prototype. Marcus, I need a hard date. When does the markdown engine ship as the primary onboarding path? Because every week it sits as a prototype is a week TaskWeaver spends as the only `.md`-based option in this space. They're not competing with our DSL — they're competing with a product we haven't shipped yet.
+
+**10. The monetization decision doesn't need more debate — it needs execution.** Priya ran the math and asked if we're aligned that self-serve is a GTM funnel, not a revenue story. I agree, and I'll add: the model I recommended — metered by workflow execution, 100 free per month, $0.10/execution above — works for both. It's free for individual developers (most won't exceed 100/month), it generates revenue from power-user teams, and it creates a natural enterprise upsell path when a team's monthly bill crosses $500 (at which point an annual committed-volume contract is cheaper for them and more predictable for us). Sarah wants a permanent free tier commitment on the record. Elena wants procurement-compatible pricing. Here's the decision: commit to 100 workflow executions/month free for individual developers, permanently. Publish the pricing page this month — transparent, self-serve, no "contact us" gate for the pro tier. Enterprise SKU with committed volume, annual prepaid, VPC deployment option. And run the model against actual usage data so we know what revenue looks like by Q4. I'm not asking you to debate this again next quarter. I'm asking you to ship it.
+
+**11. Net new workflow growth versus retention — where is revenue going to come from?** 5,200 monthly active workflows, 22% MoM growth, 12% monthly churn, 340 WAU. Some subset of those 5,200 workflows are power users who will eventually cross the 100-execution threshold and convert to paid. Who are they? I want to see a power-user cohort analysis. Of the 1,500 orgs, what's the distribution of workflow execution volume? Top 10%? Top 5%? What does the top decile's execution count look like versus the median? Because the revenue model I'm recommending depends on a power-law distribution — a small number of heavy users subsidizing a large base of free users. If the distribution is flat — if everyone runs roughly the same number of workflows — then a usage-based model doesn't work, and we need to reconsider. What does the actual execution distribution look like?
+
+**12. The bash-script loss is a GTM failure, not a product failure.** "Ash was overkill for 3 agents." That team didn't choose bash because bash is better. They chose bash because we didn't show them what 3 agents could do in Ash that bash can't. Bash doesn't give you cross-agent orchestration. Bash doesn't give you audit trails. Bash doesn't give you deterministic rollback. But we didn't tell that story — we let them evaluate the product against bash on bash's terms. At Vercel, when someone said "I can just use Netlify," we didn't argue features — we showed them a Next.js deploy with ISR, middleware, and edge functions in 60 seconds. The demo is the sales pitch. What did our demo look like for the team we lost? Did we show them a workflow that crossed 3 agents, logged every step, recovered from failure, and produced an audit trail — in 5 minutes? Or did we let them read docs and compare syntax? Because if we can't win a 3-agent bake-off against bash, we can't win a 50-agent enterprise evaluation against a $600K deal. I want the team that lost that deal to do a formal loss review, and I want the board to see the findings. Show me what we demoed.
+
+**13. Churn at 12% with 14 months of runway — what's the plan to invert the curve?** 12% monthly churn means the average user lasts about 8 months. At the current rate, half the users we acquire today will be gone by March 2027. You can't build a bottom-up GTM motion on a leaky bucket. I want to understand the churn drivers. Is it people who sign up, try once, fail, and leave? Activation churn. Is it people who use it for a month, hit a limitation, and quit? Capability churn. Is it people who switch to alternatives? Competitive churn. If we don't know which bucket is driving the 12%, we're guessing at the fix. I need a churn decomposition by next board meeting — and I need the top reason people cite when they leave. A survey. Exit interviews. Something. At 14 months of runway, churn math compounds faster than growth math.
+
+**14. Ash Cloud's delay burned trust with early adopters — what's the recovery plan?** Six weeks late, auth migration issues, 45-minute outage. Two engineers feeling "reactive." The team shipped something they felt was incomplete. At the bottom-up stage, the early adopters on Ash Cloud are your future champions, your reference customers, and your case studies. If their experience is buggy auth and 3.2% error rates, they won't champion us — they'll warn their peers. What's the concrete reliability roadmap for the next 90 days? What error rate are we committing to by end of Q3? The enterprise pipeline doesn't matter if we can't keep the free-tier users happy enough to recommend us.
+
+The board meeting is the place where I earn my seat. I've built the GTM engine at three companies that crossed $100M+ valuations. Sarah's got the community covered. Elena's got enterprise architecture. Priya's got the burn rate. My lane is the conversion math — and the conversion math right now says you're acquiring users you can't activate, routing them through a funnel you haven't instrumented, toward a pricing model you haven't shipped, for a product whose primary interface sits as a prototype while a 3-person competitor ships it to market. The window between "too early to monetize" and "too late to matter" is measured in months, not years. TaskWeaver is here. The category is forming. You have the community, the vision, and the product architecture. What you don't have — yet — is a GTM motion that converts adoption into revenue before the money runs out. That's my job to push for. Let's build the funnel, fix the conversion, keep the community, and close the enterprise deals. Not in that order — in parallel. Because the runway math doesn't give us time to do them sequentially.

 I have data gaps — activation funnels, churn drivers, contributor pathways, power-user cohorts, execution distributions, champion maps. All of them are embarrassing to admit I don't have, and all of them will be closed by the next board meeting.
 
 I'm now turning this over to strategic discussions. The board has asked hard questions and given clear direction. I want to hear your priorities for the 90 days between now and the next meeting — specifically, which of the open items (SOC 2 scoping, markdown engine ship, pricing page launch, community governance, churn decomposition, enterprise champion mapping) the board wants to see resolved first. Let's discuss.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Strategic Discussion — Monetization
+
+### Position: Marcus Thorne (CEO and Co-Founder)
+
+I came into this meeting torn. I'm leaving with a decision. Here's the model, the threshold, and the path — grounded in our actual numbers.
+
+**The model: metered by workflow execution volume.**
+
+Usage-based pricing is the only model that aligns cost with value for a platform whose core unit is *work done*, not *headcount*. We don't charge for seats — we charge for orchestrations. A two-person startup running 20 workflows a month pays nothing. A 200-person platform team running 5,000 workflows a month pays proportionally for the orchestration value they consume. That's the fundamental argument, and the board has sharpened it: self-serve Ash Cloud is a GTM funnel for enterprise, not a stand-alone revenue story at our conversion rates. Priya is right on the math — at 1.2% free-to-paid and $100/month self-serve ACV, organic signups will never close the gap against $310K/month burn. I accept that. The model must serve two masters simultaneously: it must be free enough to drive developer adoption (the top of the funnel), and it must generate meaningful revenue from power-user teams and enterprise contracts (the bottom).
+
+James's specific recommendation — 100 free workflow executions per month, $0.10 per execution above that threshold, volume discounts at 1,000 and 10,000 executions, and enterprise annual committed-volume contracts — is the right structure, and I'm adopting it. Here's why it works against our actual product data:
+
+- **5,200 monthly active workflows across 1,500 orgs.** That's an average of 3.5 executions per org per month. The overwhelming majority of our users — individual developers, small teams, open-source projects — run well under 100 executions/month. They stay free, permanently. That's the developer adoption engine Sarah demanded, and I'm committing to it on the record.
+- **The power-law question James raised is answerable even without the full distribution data.** We have 340 WAU but only 4 paying customers. The behavioral signal is that a small cohort of heavy users exists — the 22% MoM workflow growth amid 12% churn is precisely the signature of a deepening power-user base inside a shallow retention surface. Those power users are the ones who will cross the 100-execution threshold. They're also the ones whose accounts map to team adoption, which James correctly identified as the leading indicator for enterprise conversion.
+- **The free-to-paid conversion problem (1.2%) becomes addressable under this model because the upgrade trigger is automatic.** You don't convince someone to "choose the paid plan" — you show them their usage dashboard crossing 100 executions and offer: keep going for $0.10 each or commit to 500/month at a discount. The friction is not a buying decision — it's a usage reality. That's how metered models convert: they let value prove itself before the invoice arrives.
+
+**The free tier threshold: 100 workflow executions per month, permanently, for individual developers.**
+
+Why 100? Because it's generous enough that no individual developer — tinkering, learning, building side projects, running a personal CI pipeline — will ever hit a paywall. Our average org runs 3.5 workflows per month. Even our power users at 21 executions/month (the estimated average for the top decile under a power-law assumption) stay free. The threshold only binds for teams running Ash at meaningful scale — the teams that are deriving real operational value and have the budget to pay for it. That's the right line.
+
+Permanently means permanently. Sarah's demand for an irrevocable commitment is correct — the community will tolerate a paid tier; they will not tolerate a rug pull. I'll publish it in the repo's pricing page and governance docs. The community also gets the runway math: "We have 14 months. We're building something worth paying for. Here's what stays free forever, and here's why." Sarah's framing is honest, and the community deserves that honesty from the founder.
+
+**The path from adoption to revenue.**
+
+The sequence is: individual developer adoption → team adoption → power-user self-serve conversion → enterprise contract. Four stages, and we're only at stage one today. Here's how each stage works with real numbers from our inputs:
+
+**Stage 1 — Individual developer adoption (now).** 280 new signups/month, 340 WAU, 1,500 orgs. The product is free. The value proposition is: replace your Makefile, your bash scripts, your ad-hoc agent chains with a legible, version-controlled, auditable workflow. `ash discover` auto-detects workflows in your repo in 30 seconds. The markdown engine — shipping end of Q3 — eliminates the DSL learning curve. The metric that matters here is activation within 48 hours, which I don't have instrumented yet, but James is right that it's the number that tells us whether the funnel exists at all.
+
+**Stage 2 — Team adoption (next 90 days).** The signal is team invites. One developer deploys a workflow, shows a teammate, the teammate deploys, the org grows to 3.5 → 10 workflows/month and 2+ users. At this point, the question becomes: is Ash embedded in their development process? Are they running workflows from CI? Are non-engineers reading the `.md` files? This is the stage where internal champions form — the person who will later push Ash through procurement at FintechCo or DataCorp. Elena and James have correctly identified that our enterprise evaluations lack deployed champions at four of six accounts. Team adoption is the prerequisite for champion formation. I'm instrumenting the team-invite conversion rate and the multi-user org distribution for the next board meeting.
+
+**Stage 3 — Self-serve conversion (power users hitting the threshold).** When a team's usage crosses 100 executions/month, the metered model activates automatically. $0.10/execution above 100. At 200 executions/month, that's $10/month — below any corporate approval threshold, payable by credit card. At 500 executions, $40/month. The self-serve tier generates adoption intelligence: we learn which orgs are deepening usage, and that data feeds the enterprise pipeline. The 1.2% free-to-paid conversion rate is the wrong number to track — what we should track is *power-user identification rate*: what percentage of orgs cross 100 executions/month? That's the pool that self-serve converts and enterprise contracts recruit from. I will report that number starting next quarter.
+
+**Stage 4 — Enterprise contracts (Q4 2026 and beyond).** When an org's monthly Ash Cloud bill approaches $500 (roughly 5,000 executions/month, or a team running 200+ workflows daily), an annual committed-volume contract is cheaper for them and more predictable for us. The enterprise SKU supports: annual prepaid, volume discounts at tiered execution levels, VPC single-tenant deployment (4-6 months out), and a procurement-compatible pricing page. Elena's four procurement criteria — annual prepaid, volume discounts, separate on-prem/VPC SKU, self-serve pricing without "contact us" — are the specification. I'm shipping the pricing page within 30 days. The enterprise SKU won't close deals until SOC 2 lands, but it must exist before SOC 2 lands so procurement teams have something to quote.
+
+The pipeline math Elena and Priya ran — probability-weighted ACV of ~$81,500 across six accounts, covering eight days of burn — is sobering. But it's also the wrong framing for the question. The question isn't "what is expected revenue from the current pipeline?" The question is: after the markdown engine ships, context compaction lands, error rates drop below 2%, SOC 2 is in progress, and the pricing page is live — what does the pipeline look like then? The current pipeline is a leading indicator of demand, not a trailing indicator of revenue. The six enterprise evaluations tell us the demand exists. The product gaps Elena enumerated — error rate, SOC 2, RBAC, DR, single-region, SSO — tell us what we have to build before the demand converts. The monetization model tells us *how* it converts when it does.
+
+The board's priority sequence for the next 90 days, as I hear it:
+
+1. **Markdown engine ships** (end of Q3). This is the GTM answer to TaskWeaver, the onboarding answer to the bash-script loss, and the procurement answer Elena demanded. Every week it sits as a prototype costs us adoption velocity and competitive positioning.
+
+2. **Pricing page ships** (30 days). 100 free executions/month, metered pro tier, enterprise SKU. Transparent, self-serve, no "contact us" gate for pro. The community gets the commitment. Procurement gets the price. I get the data from actual usage to validate the model.
+
+3. **SOC 2 scoping begins** (30 days). Audit firm selected, budget allocated, kickoff scheduled. This is the gate on all six enterprise deals. Without it in motion, the pipeline is a wish list.
+
+4. **Context compaction ships** (August). Addresses the largest error-rate driver (truncated output at context limits) and extends reliable workflow depth to ~100 steps. Error rate target: sub-2% by end of Q3.
+
+5. **Community governance and contributor program launch** (next board meeting). CLA/DCO, maintainer nomination, @devopsjane's stipend, contributor pathway. Sarah's right that community trust is fragile. Fixing it is parallel work, not sequential.
+
+I'm not debating this model again next quarter. I'm shipping it. The board gave me a decision I couldn't make alone, and I'm grateful for the clarity — and the directness. Let's build it.

 5. **Community governance and contributor program launch** (next board meeting). CLA/DCO, maintainer nomination, @devopsjane's stipend, contributor pathway. Sarah's right that community trust is fragile. Fixing it is parallel work, not sequential.
 
 I'm not debating this model again next quarter. I'm shipping it. The board gave me a decision I couldn't make alone, and I'm grateful for the clarity — and the directness. Let's build it.
+
+### Position: Priya Nair (Investor Director, Framework Capital)
+
+Marcus, I appreciate the decision, but I'm not aligned with the model you've chosen. James's metered model — $0.10 per execution above 100 free — is elegant on a whiteboard. It doesn't survive the unit-economics math of this company at this burn rate. I'm going to explain why, and I'm going to propose a concrete alternative that I can model, fundraise against, and hold the team accountable to.
+
+**The metered model fails the math.**
+
+Let's run the numbers you owe the board. 5,200 monthly active workflows across 1,500 orgs. Average: 3.5 executions per org per month. Even if we assume an extreme power-law distribution — the top 10% of orgs run 60% of volume — that's 3,120 executions spread across 150 orgs, or roughly 21 executions per org per month in the top decile. *Nobody in the top decile crosses 100.* The metered model generates zero self-serve revenue from our current user base. Not small revenue — zero. Every single one of our 340 free users, all four paying customers, and all 1,500 orgs would stay on the free tier indefinitely at current usage levels.
+
+James asked the right question about the execution distribution, and the fact that neither of us has the data yet is why I'm objecting to a model that depends on a distribution we can't confirm. If the execution curve is flat — if 95% of orgs run under 20 workflows a month — then usage-based pricing is a rounding error against a $310K burn for quarters, potentially years. You acknowledged that at 1.2% free-to-paid and $100/month self-serve, the math doesn't close. Metered at $0.10 per execution doesn't fix that — it makes the unit of revenue smaller, not larger. The problem isn't the denomination. It's that our users don't run enough workflows yet.
+
+**The Atlassian model: per-seat pricing with a permanently free individual tier.**
+
+My seat on this board exists to ensure the company survives long enough to build the category. That means the pricing model must generate meaningful revenue from the customers who can pay — not from a hypothetical power-law distribution we haven't validated. Here's the model:
+
+- **Free forever: Individual developers.** One seat. Full product. No execution caps. No feature gates. No metered anxiety. The free tier is a person, not a usage limit — because we want individual developers to use Ash as much as possible. Every execution they run builds the standard, trains the model ecosystem, and creates the workflow IP that makes Ash sticky. Sarah's irrevocable commitment applies here, and I endorse it fully. No rug pull.
+
+- **Team tier: $29/seat/month, starting at 5 seats.** When a team invites a second developer, they enter a 14-day trial. After that, it's $29 per seat per month, minimum 5 seats ($145/month). This is not a usage gate — it's a collaboration gate. The value we unlock at team scale is shared workflow libraries, team-level audit trails, and CI integration. That's worth orders of magnitude more than per-execution pennies. At 5 seats, $145/month covers 1,450 metered executions — more than our top-decile org runs today. The team tier captures value from the organizational surface area, not the execution count.
+
+- **Enterprise tier: Custom ACV starting at $60K/year.** This is what closes the runway math. Per-seat pricing at $29/seat/month for 200 seats is $69,600/year — the entry point for our $60K+ enterprise pipeline. Above 500 seats, volume discounts. The enterprise SKU includes: SSO, RBAC, audit trail exports (Article 14 compliance), VPC single-tenant deployment, and annual prepaid contracts. This is the SKU that passes Elena's procurement test — it fits a standard purchase order, it's per-seat (the format every procurement team understands), and it has a published price.
+
+**Why per-seat beats metered — by the numbers.**
+
+Take the 1,500 orgs currently on the platform. Under metered at $0.10/execution with 100 free, monthly revenue: approximately $0. Under per-seat, if just 5% of those orgs (75) have 2+ developers and convert to the team tier at 5 seats each, that's $10,875/month. That's 27x our current MRR from a conversion rate we can actually model.
+
+The metered model forces us to wait for usage depth we don't have. The per-seat model converts on organizational adoption, which is the signal James correctly identified as the enterprise precursor. When a team has 2+ developers on Ash, they've already made the organizational commitment. Charging at that point aligns pricing with the moment value is realized — not with an arbitrary execution counter that most users never hit.
+
+**The path from adoption to revenue, restated for per-seat.**
+
+Stage 1 — Individual adoption (now, free): Everything Marcus described. `ash discover`, markdown engine, zero-config onboarding. One developer, full product, unlimited executions, free forever. This is the top of the funnel and the community trust anchor.
+
+Stage 2 — Team adoption triggers the trial (next 90 days): When a second developer joins an org, a 14-day team trial activates. During the trial, the team sees shared workflow dashboards, team audit trails, and CI integration — the features that make Ash an organizational tool, not a personal one. At the end of 14 days, the org chooses: stay on the free individual tier (losing team features but keeping individual access) or convert to the team tier at $29/seat/month with a 5-seat minimum. The metric we track: team trial conversion rate. Not the 1.2% free-to-paid from a self-serve pricing page nobody visits — but the conversion rate of teams who experience the team features and decide they're worth paying for.
+
+Stage 3 — Enterprise upsell (Q4 2026 and beyond): When an org hits 20+ seats (roughly $7,000/month), the enterprise sales motion engages. The solutions engineer James recommended — replacing the AE — runs technical evaluations, builds champion relationships, and maps the deployment to the enterprise SKU. The enterprise close is per-seat annual, with deployment, compliance, and support add-ons.
+
+**Responding to Marcus's adoption of the metered model.**
+
+Marcus, you said the metered model "lets value prove itself before the invoice arrives." That's the right principle applied to the wrong unit. Workflow execution count does not measure value — it measures activity. A team running 20 high-stakes deployment workflows a month derives more value than a team running 200 experimental runs. Per-execution pricing charges proportional to activity, not value. Per-seat pricing charges proportional to the number of people whose work depends on the platform — which is the closest proxy we have for organizational value captured.
+
+You also said the metered model converts because "the upgrade trigger is automatic — you show them their usage dashboard crossing 100." But if our top decile runs 21 executions a month, nobody sees that dashboard. The trigger never fires. The automatic conversion story depends on usage depth we simply don't have today, and I can't plan a revenue model against a hypothetical future usage curve that may never materialize.
+
+**The unit-economics test.**
+
+I told the board in my questions that I will block any pricing model that needs 10,000 paying users to reach break-even. At $310K/month burn, break-even on self-serve alone would be:
+
+- Metered: $310,000 / ($0.10 × avg overage of, say, 200 executions) = 15,500 power users crossing the threshold. At 1.2% free-to-paid, that's 1.3 million free users. The market doesn't exist.
+- Per-seat team tier: $310,000 / ($145/month × conversion rate of, say, 5% of orgs) = need 2,138 converting teams. At 1,500 orgs today and 280 signups/month, that's a faster convergence — but still requires scale. Which is why enterprise is the revenue story and team tier is the bridge.
+
+The enterprise pipeline is the only path to break-even in the runway window. The team tier isn't designed to close the gap — it's designed to validate that organizations will pay for Ash before we ask procurement departments to write $60K checks. A team paying $145/month is a qualified enterprise lead with demonstrated willingness to pay. A team running 200 free executions a month is not.
+
+**Concrete recommendation.**
+
+1. **Free tier:** One developer, unlimited workflows, full product, forever. No execution caps. The person is the unit.
+2. **Team tier:** $29/seat/month, minimum 5 seats ($145/month), triggered by multi-user org adoption. 14-day trial. Includes team features (shared dashboards, team audit trails, CI integration).
+3. **Enterprise tier:** Per-seat annual, starting at 200 seats ($69,600/year), with volume discounts above 500 seats. Includes SSO, RBAC, VPC deployment, audit trail exports, priority support. Published pricing, procurement-compatible.
+4. **Pricing page:** Ship within 30 days. Free (1 seat), Team ($29/seat, 5 min), Enterprise (contact for 200+). A procurement analyst can quote the per-seat number in 60 seconds.
+
+The metered model is a Vercel play for a company with Vercel's usage depth. We don't have Vercel's usage depth. We have a standard-setting motion with shallow per-org engagement and a deepening power-user cohort that still doesn't run enough volume to justify metered billing. Per-seat captures organizational commitment at the moment it forms — which is earlier than usage depth, more predictable, and procurement-compatible. That's the model I can model, fundraise against, and hold the company accountable to. I'm asking Marcus to revise the pricing page to per-seat before it ships.

Position appended. Here's a summary of what was added:

**Section 3 — Strategic Discussion: Monetization → Position: Priya Nair (Investor Director, Framework Capital)**

Priya argues against the metered model Marcus endorsed (James's proposal of $0.10/execution above 100 free) and advocates for a **per-seat Atlassian model** instead:

- **Free tier:** 1 developer, unlimited workflows, forever — no execution caps, no metered anxiety. The person is the unit.
- **Team tier:** $29/seat/month, 5-seat minimum ($145/month), triggered by multi-user org adoption with a 14-day trial, gated on collaboration features (shared dashboards, team audit trails, CI integration).
- **Enterprise tier:** Per-seat annual starting at 200 seats ($69,600/year), with volume discounts above 500.

Her core argument is unit-economics grounded in the actual data: the top decile runs ~21 executions/month — nobody crosses the 100-execution threshold, so the metered model generates $0 from the current base. By contrast, if 5% of the 1,500 orgs have 2+ developers and convert to the team tier, that's ~$10,875/month — 27x current MRR. She directly responds to Marcus's "value proves itself before the invoice" framing, arguing that per-execution charges for activity, not value — per-seat charges for the number of people whose work depends on the platform.
