 That's the Ash I joined this board to build. Not a product with a defensive license. A project with an offensive community. The license is the permission. The community is the power. Let's keep both, and let's stop debating the one that was never the problem.
+
+### Position: Dr. Elena Vasquez (Independent Director, Enterprise Architecture)
+
+I'm the fourth director to tell you MIT stays. I'm also the one who will tell you why the other three arguments — as correct as they are — don't cover the thing that matters most to me: procurement. I've spent nine years on the architecture review board at Deutsche Bank. I have personally rejected vendors because of their license. Not their product. Not their pricing. Not their security posture. Their license. I've never had to justify that rejection to anyone above me, because the bank's legal department maintained an approved-license registry, and any product using a non-OSI license was rejected automatically — not after review, not after risk assessment, automatically. BSL is not on that registry. It never will be. And the procurement teams at FintechCo, DataCorp, CloudServe, BankCo, MedTech, and RetailGiants operate the same registry. So when I say BSL kills enterprise, I am not speculating. I am describing a procurement rejection pattern I executed personally, against vendors with products significantly more mature than Ash, with revenue multiples we don't have, and with sales teams who had actual relationships inside the bank. We rejected them anyway. The license was sufficient. Nothing else needed to be wrong.
+
+I need this board — and Marcus in particular — to understand what that rejection looks like from inside the room, because I don't think any of you have seen it.
+
+**How enterprise license rejection actually works.**
+
+At Deutsche Bank, every software vendor went through a 120-point assessment. It wasn't one assessment. It was a pipeline: architecture review, security review, legal review, procurement review. The architecture team evaluated the product. The security team evaluated the posture. The legal team evaluated the contract — and the license. The procurement team compiled the purchase order. Four gates. Sequential. A failure at any gate stopped the evaluation. The vendor was not informed of *which* gate failed unless they asked — and by the time they asked, the internal champion who had sponsored the evaluation had already been told, internally, that the vendor was delisted.
+
+The license gate was gate two — legal review. Before anyone evaluated whether the product solved the bank's problem at a price the bank could afford, someone on the legal team checked the license against the approved registry. MIT: pass. Apache 2.0: pass. BSD: pass. GPLv2: pass with restrictions. AGPL: fail. BSL: fail. BUSL: fail. Any source-available license with a use restriction: fail. The registry was maintained by the bank's IP counsel and updated annually. It was not negotiable. It could not be overridden by a VP. It could not be waived by a business case. The bank did not negotiate individual license terms with software vendors — it maintained a list of pre-approved licenses, and if a vendor's license wasn't on it, the evaluation stopped.
+
+I want everyone in this room to sit with that for a moment. The license is not a detail in the procurement process. It is a binary gate. It is checked *before* the product is evaluated. If you fail the license gate, nobody at the bank ever sees your security architecture whitepaper. Nobody evaluates your RBAC model. Nobody opens your pricing page. You are rejected at step two of a four-step process, and you may never know why, because the champion who brought you in is embarrassed to tell you their legal team killed the deal before she ever got to present it.
+
+BSL would put Ash on the wrong side of that gate. Every one of our six enterprise evaluations — all of which are already stalled at security review — would be killed at legal review before anyone evaluated whether the product solves their problem. The $600K pipeline, the $81,500 probability-weighted ACV, the two named executive sponsors who have deployed Ash in dev — all of it evaporates at a step most founders don't even know exists. I've watched it happen. I've done it myself. And I am telling you: do not make me watch it happen to Ash.
+
+**Responding to Marcus's fear — because it's real, and the procurement answer is different from the community answer.**
+
+Marcus, you said in your opening that TaskWeaver launching a `.md`-based competitor changed your calculus on licensing. I understand why. It is infuriating to watch a 3-person YC team take your format — the format you invented, the format the market is telling you is the right one — and run faster with it. Priya is right that a license change doesn't stop them. Sarah is right that the community cost is permanent and disproportionate. James is right that the moat is adoption depth, not a legal clause. But I want to add something none of them said, because they haven't sat on the buyer's side of the table — and I have, for nine years.
+
+The reason TaskWeaver is terrifying is not that they forked our format. The reason they are terrifying is that they validated our format. They told the market — 1,200 stars in two weeks — that the `.md`-based orchestration standard is the right one, and that Ash is the project that invented it. That is not a licensing threat. That is a market validation. The worst thing that could have happened wasn't TaskWeaver shipping a clone. The worst thing would have been if *nobody* shipped a clone — if Ash's format was so uninteresting that even a 3-person YC team didn't bother. The clone is evidence that we built something worth copying. The question isn't whether to defend it with a license. The question is whether we can out-execute the clones before they capture the adoption.
+
+And the ironic thing — the thing I need this board to absorb — is that the license change you were considering would *help* TaskWeaver. If Ash goes BSL, TaskWeaver becomes the only MIT-licensed `.md` orchestration tool in the market. They become the "open" option. They scoop up the contributors we lose. They capture the enterprise evaluations we get delisted from. They inherit the community trust we fracture. The BSL doesn't defend us from TaskWeaver — it hands them our market position. It's not a shield. It's a surrender.
+
+**Responding to Priya: capital efficiency is correct, but you understated the enterprise cost.**
+
+Priya, you priced the BSL relicense at $50K–$100K in legal fees plus engineering time plus community trust erosion. That's correct — for the cost of executing the license change. But the enterprise cost is larger than you calculated, and I want to put a number on it.
+
+The probability-weighted ACV of our enterprise pipeline is $81,500 — your math, not mine, and I think it's generous. But that is the expected value of the *current* pipeline under the *current* license. Under BSL, the expected value is zero. Not reduced. Zero. Because every single one of those six accounts has a legal department with an approved-license registry that rejects BSL. So the cost of a license change is not $50K–$100K in legal fees plus some undefined community trust erosion. It is $50K–$100K in cash, plus the complete destruction of the enterprise pipeline, plus the permanent loss of contributors, plus the market-position gift to TaskWeaver. The total cost of a BSL relicense, measured in cash, pipeline, and community, exceeds the company's current cash on hand. You cannot afford it. At 14 months of runway and $4.4 million in the bank, this company cannot afford to destroy its own enterprise pipeline on purpose.
+
+Priya, you said you would block any BSL proposal before $10M ARR. I support that threshold. I want it on the record that I support a higher one: $50M ARR and a named enterprise customer base of at least 50 Fortune 500 accounts. Because at $50M ARR, the enterprise customers who already depend on Ash will have procurement departments that understand the license and have accepted it as part of their contract. The legal question shifts from "is this license on the approved registry?" to "is this license acceptable under our existing vendor agreement?" That's the threshold where a license conversation becomes survivable. Below it, the conversation is not about defense — it's about self-harm.
+
+**Responding to Sarah: the HashiCorp lesson is correct, and I lived it from the other side.**
+
+Sarah, you described the BSL relicense from inside HashiCorp. I want to describe it from inside the enterprise that evaluated the fork. Because when OpenTofu launched, every bank, every hospital, every government contractor that used Terraform had the same conversation: do we stay on HashiCorp's BSL Terraform, or do we migrate to the Linux Foundation's OpenTofu fork?
+
+At Deutsche Bank, that conversation took about four hours. The legal team confirmed that BSL was not on the approved registry. The architecture team confirmed that OpenTofu was a drop-in replacement for the bank's current Terraform usage. The procurement team confirmed that OpenTofu was MIT-licensed and free. The decision was made before lunch. The bank migrated. HashiCorp lost a customer with thousands of seats, and they lost them not because OpenTofu was better — it wasn't, in those early weeks — but because OpenTofu was MIT-licensed and HashiCorp was not.
+
+That's the procurement dynamic you haven't named: when a BSL relicense triggers a fork, the fork inherits the MIT license. The fork becomes the enterprise-compatible option. The original project becomes the enterprise liability. HashiCorp survived this because they had a billion dollars in revenue and an installed base so deep that many enterprises couldn't migrate even if they wanted to. Ash does not have a billion dollars in revenue. Ash has $400 in MRR and 1,500 orgs running an average of 3.5 workflows apiece. If we trigger a fork — and we would trigger a fork, because every BSL relicense of a popular MIT project triggers a fork — the fork inherits the MIT license, the enterprise pipeline migrates to the fork, and we are left with a source-available product nobody can buy and a community that just forked us. That's not a strategy. That's the end of the company.
+
+**What I'm actually afraid of — because Marcus was honest, and this room should be too.**
+
+Marcus named his fear: absorption by a bigger player. Priya named hers: capital destruction at a stage where every dollar counts. Sarah named hers: losing the contributor trust that makes Ash a project, not a product. Here's mine.
+
+I'm afraid that Ash will close zero enterprise deals — not because the product isn't good enough, but because we never got it past the procurement gate. I'm afraid that six months from now, at the next board meeting, the enterprise pipeline looks exactly like it does today: six evaluations, zero revenue, all stalled at security review. Except by then, SOC 2 won't have started, the error rate will still be above 3%, the audit trail won't be Article 14 compliant, and we'll be having the same conversation with six fewer months of runway. The licensing question was a distraction from that fear. The per-seat-versus-metered debate was a necessary debate, but also a distraction from it. The DSL question was a distraction from it. I am afraid that we are spending our board meetings debating things that won't matter if the enterprise pipeline never converts — and the enterprise pipeline will never convert if the product isn't enterprise-ready.
+
+The license is the easiest thing to get right. MIT. Done. Permanent. Codified. That's one line in a GOVERNANCE.md file. The hard things — SOC 2 Type II, RBAC, audit trail compliance, DR planning, multi-region architecture, error rate reduction from 3.2% to 0.5%, enterprise SSO, VPC deployment, the markdown engine — those take months of engineering effort, dedicated hires, budget allocation, and sustained execution. Every hour this board spends debating licensing is an hour it doesn't spend holding Marcus accountable to the hard things. I want to redirect this conversation — not to "what license should we use?" but to "what are we shipping this quarter that makes Ash purchasable by a regulated enterprise?" Because the license is already the right answer. The rest of the product isn't there yet.
+
+**My position — and the standard I'm setting for the enterprise seat on this board.**
+
+MIT stays. Permanently. Codified in GOVERNANCE.md as irrevocable. This is not up for review. This is not conditional on reaching a revenue threshold. This is the license under which Ash was built, under which 94 contributors contributed, under which 1,500 orgs adopted it, and under which all six enterprise evaluations opened. To change it now — at $400 MRR, with a 3-person competitor validating our format and an enterprise pipeline that hasn't converted a single dollar — would be the most expensive unilateral decision this company could make that doesn't involve terminating the engineering team.
+
+But I want to be clear about what "MIT stays" does and does not mean. It means the license is the right tool. It does not mean the license is the moat. The moat — the thing that makes Ash unbundleable by a big platform, the thing that makes TaskWeaver irrelevant, the thing that makes an enterprise write a $150K check — is the following five things, and I want the board to hold Marcus accountable to every one of them between now and our next meeting:
+
+1. **SOC 2 Type II scoping begins within 30 days.** Audit firm selected. Budget approved. Kickoff scheduled. Without SOC 2 in motion, the enterprise pipeline is a wish list. Marcus committed to this in his CEO response. I will verify it at the next meeting, and I will verify it by asking for the audit firm's name and the kickoff date — not a status update.
+
+2. **Error rate drops below 2% by end of Q3, with a scoped path to 0.5%.** Context compaction ships in August. Output schema validation detects format errors deterministically. A dedicated reliability rotation owns the metric. The 3.2% error rate is the number I would cite if I were on the other side of the procurement table recommending rejection. Get it down.
+
+3. **EU AI Act Article 14 audit trail compliance ships by January 2027.** Confidence scoring on every agent output. Decision rationale logged at every branch point. Human-override events with identity, timestamp, and justification. The enforcement date is non-negotiable. If we miss it, every enterprise account subject to EU regulation — all six of them — cannot legally deploy Ash in production.
+
+4. **RBAC ships in Q4 with creator, approver, operator role separation.** Without it, SOC 2 change management criteria fail. Without SOC 2, enterprise deals die. This is a dependency chain, not a feature wishlist.
+
+5. **The pricing page ships within 30 days, per-seat, procurement-quotable.** A fixed per-seat number a procurement analyst can multiply by headcount and submit in a purchase order within 60 seconds. No estimation required. No "contact us" gate on any self-serve tier.
+
+Those five items — not the license, not the DSL, not even the markdown engine — are the things that determine whether Ash closes enterprise revenue before the cash runs out. The markdown engine matters for adoption velocity and competitive positioning against TaskWeaver. But adoption without enterprise conversion is a vanity metric at 14 months of runway. I am not here to validate adoption numbers. I am here to make sure the adoption converts.
+
+**Closing.**
+
+I rejected more vendor deals in nine years at Deutsche Bank than most enterprise sales teams close in a career. The rejections that still bother me are the ones where the product was excellent and the vendor was killed by something trivial — a license clause in the wrong registry, a missing certification, a pricing page that required the buyer to estimate something they didn't know. Those rejections weren't about the product. They were about the procurement interface — the friction between the vendor's willingness to sell and the buyer's ability to buy. The license is the first piece of that interface. Get it wrong, and nothing else matters. Get it right — MIT, permanent, irrevocable — and you still have to get the certification, the error rate, the audit trail, the RBAC, and the pricing page right too. But at least the door is open.
+
+Marcus, you said in your licensing position that you're emotionally attached to open-source purity. I'm not emotionally attached to anything in this room except the outcome. MIT is the right license not because open-source is pure, but because it is the only license under which a Fortune 500 company can legally purchase this product without a legal review that will kill the deal. That's not philosophy. That's procurement. Keep the license. Build the product. Close the deals. That's the enterprise seat's guidance. I expect to see progress on all five items at the next board meeting — not promises, progress. The clock is running.

[0m

Now I have all the context. Let me append Elena's licensing position.
Elena's position has been appended under Section 4. Her argument centers on three points:

1. **Enterprise procurement reality** — Drawing on her nine years at Deutsche Bank's architecture review board, she describes how approved-license registries work as binary gates that reject non-OSI licenses automatically, before any product evaluation occurs.

2. **The BSL paradox** — A license change wouldn't protect Ash from TaskWeaver; it would hand TaskWeaver the "MIT-licensed open option" market position, gift them the contributors Ash would lose, and kill Ash's own enterprise pipeline.

3. **The real moat** — She redirects from licensing to five concrete deliverables: SOC 2, error rate reduction to sub-2%, Article 14 audit trails, RBAC, and a procurement-quotable pricing page. She commits to holding Marcus accountable to all five at the next meeting.

Be honest about fears and trade-offs. This is a board, not a press release.

I expect to see progress on all five items at the next board meeting — not promises, progress. The clock is running.
+
+### Position: James Okonkwo (Independent Director, DevTools Go-To-Market)
+
+I'm the fifth voice in a room where four directors have already told you MIT stays. I'm not going to repeat their arguments — but I am going to tell you why the GTM lens makes those arguments not just correct, but existential. And I'm going to be honest about something I haven't said in this meeting yet: I lost the monetization vote 3-2, and I think the licensing conversation is connected to that loss in a way this board hasn't fully confronted.
+
+**The Vercel lesson that applies directly here.**
+
+At Vercel, Next.js was MIT-licensed from day one. It still is. It was the foundation of a $2.5B valuation, not in spite of being MIT, but *because* it was MIT. The license removed every barrier to adoption. A developer could `npm install next`, deploy a project, and never think about licensing — ever. That frictionless adoption was the engine that produced the bottom-up flywheel: individual developers → team adoption → enterprise procurement. The license wasn't a detail in that flywheel. It was the fuel.
+
+I watched competitors fork Next.js. I watched Gatsby, Remix, SvelteKit — project after project — take pieces of our architecture and build competing frameworks. Some of them were faster. Some of them had better DX for specific use cases. None of them stopped Vercel from building the business. Because the moat wasn't the framework code — it was the adoption. When a team committed their Next.js config to a repo, wrote their middleware layer, built their ISR strategy, trained their frontend engineers on the routing model — they weren't switching to Remix because Remix shipped a slightly better `getServerSideProps`. The switching cost was operational, not legal. The workflows were the moat.
+
+That's Ash. That's exactly Ash. TaskWeaver copied the `.md` format. Copilot has a YAML workflow engine. Anthropic has MCP. Four YC companies are entering the category. None of them can copy the 5,200 workflows already embedded in 1,500 orgs. None of them can copy the habit — the developer who reaches for `ash discover` before writing a bash script, the team that puts `.ash` files in their CI pipeline, the PM who reads the workflow file and understands what the agents are doing. That's the moat. Not a legal clause. The adoption.
+
+And this is where I want to connect the licensing conversation to the monetization vote I just lost — because I think they're linked, and I think the board needs to see the combined risk.
+
+**The connected risk nobody has named: per-seat + BSL is a double-barreled adoption killer.**
+
+I argued in the monetization discussion that per-seat pricing taxes the behavior we most need: team formation. When a developer knows inviting a teammate triggers a pricing clock, they self-censor. The viral invite loop breaks. The bottom-up motion — "one developer discovers, shows a teammate, teammate deploys, org grows, SSO gets asked for" — depends on zero friction at the point of team growth. Per-seat puts a price on the invite. I lost that argument 3-2. I accept the board's decision. I will track the team-invite conversion rates and the trial-to-paid conversion rates as Marcus committed, and I will bring the data back to the next meeting.
+
+But here's what I need this board to understand: if you had voted to change the license *on top of* voting for per-seat, you would not have been adding one friction to the adoption flywheel. You would have been adding two frictions that compound. A developer considering Ash would face: (1) "If I invite my teammate, I might trigger a $87/month billing event" AND (2) "And the license might change again, because it changed once already." That's not a pricing model and a license. That's a trust withdrawal and an adoption tax applied simultaneously. The combined effect would suppress the bottom-up motion faster than either friction alone — and at 1,500 orgs, 3.5 workflows per org, and 12% monthly churn, we cannot afford to suppress adoption from two directions at once.
+
+The fact that this board voted for per-seat but unanimously rejected BSL is the right outcome — but it's a fragile one. Because the developer who hesitates to invite a teammate because of the trial cliff is the same developer who would have walked away entirely if the license had changed. The per-seat model already introduces friction at the team-formation threshold. Adding a license change on top would have been catastrophic. I'm relieved the board saw that. I'm less confident the board sees that the per-seat friction alone is a real risk to the adoption flywheel — but that's a debate for the next meeting, with data, not for this one.
+
+**Responding to Priya: you're right about capital efficiency, and I want to add the GTM cost you didn't price.**
+
+Priya, you priced the BSL relicense at $50K–$100K in legal fees, plus engineering time, plus community trust erosion, plus enterprise pipeline destruction. That's a six-figure cost on a company with $4.4 million in the bank and 14 months of runway. The math is correct. But I want to add a cost that your financial lens might not capture, because it's measured in something you can't put on a balance sheet: the bottom-up GTM flywheel is the most capital-efficient customer acquisition engine this company will ever have, and a license change would damage it in a way that requires *paid* GTM to repair.
+
+Let me explain what I mean. Right now, Ash acquires users at effectively $0 CAC — organic Hacker News front pages, community word-of-mouth, GitHub stars converting to signups, `ash discover` creating zero-config onboarding. That's not a happy accident. That's the MIT license working as a GTM engine. Because the license is open, developers trust us enough to try us. Because the license is open, contributors write tutorials and answer forum questions and defend us in comment threads — for free. Because the license is open, community members like @devopsjane submit 9 PRs in a quarter and carry community ops on their back — for free. That labor — the tutorials, the forum answers, the PR reviews, the HN comments, the word-of-mouth advocacy — is the engine that produces 280 signups a month at zero marginal cost.
+
+If you change the license, you don't just lose the contributors Sarah described. You lose the *acquisition channel* those contributors powered. The tutorials stop. The forum answers dry up. The HN comment threads turn negative. The word-of-mouth flips from advocacy to warning. And suddenly, 280 organic signups a month becomes... 50? 20? And to make up the gap, you need paid GTM — advertising, paid content, developer relations staff, conference sponsorships. The marketing budget that this board just reallocated from $12K to $0 for paid awareness? You'd need to bring it back — at $20K or $30K a month — just to replace the organic acquisition engine the license change destroyed. The cost of a BSL relicense, measured in GTM terms, is not just the legal fees. It's the capital you have to spend to rebuild the funnel you just broke. At 14 months of runway, we cannot afford to buy what MIT gives us for free.
+
+**Responding to Sarah: the HashiCorp lesson is correct, and I want to add the timing dimension.**
+
+Sarah, you described the BSL relicense from inside the room. I want to add something I observed from the outside, because it matters for Ash's specific stage. The Terraform BSL relicense happened in August 2023. HashiCorp went public in December 2021. At the time of the relicense, they had over $500M in annual revenue, a public-company balance sheet, thousands of enterprise customers, and an installed base so deep that even the OpenTofu fork couldn't dislodge most of them. They could afford to lose contributors because they had revenue to replace community labor with paid staff. They could afford to lose community trust because they had enterprise contracts that weren't going anywhere. They could afford the fork because the fork was a nuisance, not an existential threat. And even with all of that — billions in market cap, hundreds of millions in revenue, a decade of community trust built up — the damage was permanent.
+
+Ash has $400 in MRR. Eight thousand GitHub stars. Ninety-four contributors. Fifteen percent repeat contributor rate. Twelve percent monthly churn. Fourteen months of runway. If HashiCorp — at 1,000x our revenue, 100x our installed base, and 50x our runway — permanently damaged their community with a license change, what do you think happens to us? The question answers itself. We don't survive it. Not because the license change is philosophically wrong — though I think it is — but because we are too small, too early, and too dependent on organic community growth to absorb the blast radius. The HashiCorp lesson for Ash isn't "don't change the license." It's "you don't have the revenue to survive the license change you're considering." Sarah is right about what happened. I'm adding: HashiCorp survived it because of scale we don't have. We wouldn't.
+
+**Responding to Elena: procurement is the gate I've been on the other side of.**
+
+Elena, you described killing vendor deals at Deutsche Bank because of license non-compliance. I've been on the other side of that table — at GitHub, at Atlassian, at Vercel — trying to close enterprise deals. And I want to confirm something you said, because it's the thing that keeps enterprise sales leaders up at night: when a vendor is delisted at the legal review gate, you often never find out why.
+
+You said it perfectly: the champion who sponsored the evaluation is embarrassed to tell you their legal team killed the deal. I've had champions go dark on me for weeks — not responding to emails, not taking calls — and when I finally got them on the phone, they admitted the deal was dead because Legal flagged a term in the contract. Not the product. Not the pricing. Not the security posture. A term. And by the time I found out, the internal budget had been reallocated, the evaluation window had closed, and the champion's political capital had been spent on a deal that couldn't close. You don't recover from that. You don't fix the term and reopen the deal. The deal is dead, and you're the last to know.
+
+A BSL relicense would create exactly that dynamic for all six of our enterprise evaluations — simultaneously. FintechCo's VP of Engineering would go dark. DataCorp's Director of Platform Engineering would stop returning calls. BankCo's procurement team would delist us automatically without anyone telling us. And we would burn six months of runway waiting for deals to close that are already dead at a gate we created ourselves. That's not a licensing strategy. That's a pipeline execution failure dressed up as a defensive legal posture. I've lost deals at the legal-review gate before, and it's the most helpless feeling in enterprise sales — because by the time you understand what happened, there's nothing you can do about it. I don't want to watch Marcus experience that for six accounts simultaneously because of a decision this board made.
+
+**What I'm actually afraid of — and why I'm still afraid even though the board voted unanimously.**
+
+I told Marcus in my board questions that TaskWeaver is a GTM problem, not a licensing problem. I stand by that. But I want to be honest about a fear I haven't fully stated: I'm afraid that the GTM problem is bigger than the markdown engine.
+
+TaskWeaver launching with 1,200 stars in two weeks validated something uncomfortable: the `.md` format — which we invented, but haven't shipped as a product — is the thing the market wants. And right now, TaskWeaver is the only company shipping it as a product. We have a prototype. They have a launched product. That gap — between "prototype" and "launched" — is the actual competitive threat, and it has nothing to do with the license. TaskWeaver didn't need our code. They needed the *idea* — the idea that agent orchestration should be expressed in markdown files that anyone can read, version, and audit. They took that idea and shipped it faster than we did. The license wouldn't have stopped them, because they didn't fork our codebase. They forked our product concept.
+
+That's the fear the board should be channeling into action. Not "what if someone copies our code?" — they already copied the format, and the format is MIT, and the format is out there, and we can't un-open it. The actual question is: "what are we shipping in the next 90 days that makes a developer choose Ash over TaskWeaver?" My answer — which I gave in the monetization discussion and which I'm putting here because it's the GTM answer to the licensing question — is:
+
+1. **Ship the markdown engine as the primary product interface by end of Q3.** Not a prototype. Not a preview. The product. The thing TaskWeaver already has. The thing developers see first when they discover Ash. If a developer tries both tools in September and TaskWeaver has a faster `.md` onboarding experience than we do, the license type is irrelevant — we lose on product, not on legal.
+
+2. **Make "first workflow in 5 minutes" the metric, not "first workflow in 48 hours."** TaskWeaver's entire surface area is a `.md` file and a runner. Their onboarding is measured in seconds, not minutes. If Ash requires a developer to install a CLI, learn a DSL, configure agents, and then optionally discover the markdown engine — we lose on onboarding time, which is the most important conversion metric at the bottom-up stage. The markdown engine eliminates the DSL learning curve. `ash discover` eliminates the configuration burden. Together, they should produce a "first workflow in 5 minutes" experience that beats TaskWeaver on time-to-value. That's the GTM moat. Not a license.
+
+3. **Build the contributor flywheel faster than TaskWeaver builds theirs.** 94 contributors at 15% repeat rate is not a community — it's a rotating door. Sarah's right about that. The contributor who submits one PR and never comes back is not a moat. The contributor who submits 10 PRs, becomes a maintainer, answers forum questions, and defends Ash in HN threads — that's a moat. TaskWeaver has 1,200 stars and probably zero repeat contributors because they launched two weeks ago. If we can convert our existing 94 contributors into a community with 25-30% repeat rates, maintainer pathways, and real governance, we build a contributor flywheel that a 3-person YC team cannot replicate — not with a faster runner, not with a better license, not with anything. The contributor flywheel is the hardest thing to build in open-source and the most valuable thing once it exists. We have the head start. Let's extend it.
+
+4. **Use the enterprise pipeline as a moat, not just a revenue target.** Elena made this point in the monetization discussion and I want to echo it here: SOC 2, RBAC, Article 14 audit trails, VPC deployment — these are organizational assets that a 3-person YC team cannot replicate quickly. The license is not the enterprise moat. The compliance posture is. Every dollar and engineering-hour we invest in SOC 2 and enterprise features is a dollar and hour that widens the gap between Ash (enterprise-ready) and TaskWeaver (not). The license doesn't widen that gap. The product does. Invest in the product.
+
+**My position: MIT stays. The adoption flywheel is the only thing that matters.**
+
+MIT stays. Permanently. Codified in GOVERNANCE.md as irrevocable. I make this recommendation not because I share Marcus's emotional attachment to open-source purity — I don't. I make it because I've built three bottom-up developer GTM motions, and all three of them depended on a license that removed every barrier between a developer's curiosity and their first deployment. MIT says: try it. Ship it. Depend on it. Contribute to it. No lawyer. No compliance check. No fear. That's the permission structure that produced 8,200 stars, 94 contributors, 1,500 orgs, and 5,200 monthly active workflows — on $12K of marketing spend. That's the most capital-efficient GTM engine this company will ever have. You don't break that engine when you have 14 months of runway and a competitor standing there with the same license and a faster product.
+
+The adoption flywheel is the strategy. The markdown engine is the product that accelerates it. The community governance model is the structure that sustains it. The enterprise compliance posture is the moat that makes switching away from it organizationally painful. The license is the permission that lets all of it happen at zero friction. Keep the permission. Ship the product. Build the community. Close the enterprise deals. In that order. In parallel. Before the money runs out.
+
+**One thing I want on the record, separate from the license — because I think it matters more than anyone in this room has said.**
+
+We spent a significant portion of this board meeting debating a license change for a 3-person competitor who didn't fork our code. They forked our *format* — the `.md` file structure that we invented but haven't shipped. That format is already MIT-licensed. It's in the wild. TaskWeaver already has it. Changing the license on the execution engine wouldn't reclaim the format — and the format is the valuable asset, not the engine. The engine is replaceable. Our own CEO just told the board he's deprioritizing the engine (the `.ash` parser) in favor of the format (the markdown engine). If the company's own engineering strategy says the engine is a compile target and the format is the product, then protecting the engine with a license change is protecting the wrong asset. The asset is the format. The format is open. The format was always going to be open — it's a markdown file. You can't close-source a markdown file. The licensing conversation was a category error from the start.
+
+I'm saying this not to embarrass Marcus — he's been more honest in this meeting than most CEOs are in a decade of board meetings, and he deserves credit for bringing fears to the table instead of hiding them behind presentation slides. I'm saying it because I want the board to internalize the lesson: the next time a competitor scares us, the question isn't "how do we protect the code?" The question is "how do we out-ship them?" Because in a market where the format is MIT-licensed markdown and the execution engine is being demoted to a compile target, the code is not the thing we're defending. The adoption is. The community is. The habit is. The workflows are. Let's defend those — with velocity, with governance, with enterprise features — and stop pretending the license is a weapon when it's actually the permission structure that lets the weapon be built.

- **MIT stays, permanently.** Rooted in Vercel's Next.js lesson — MIT was the fuel for the $2.5B bottom-up flywheel, not an obstacle to it.

+++ /opt/apps/agents/ash/workflows/board/board-output/2026-07-09/meeting-minutes.md
@@ -1289,4 +1289,108 @@
 
 We spent a significant portion of this board meeting debating a license change for a 3-person competitor who didn't fork our code. They forked our *format* — the `.md` file structure that we invented but haven't shipped. That format is already MIT-licensed. It's in the wild. TaskWeaver already has it. Changing the license on the execution engine wouldn't reclaim the format — and the format is the valuable asset, not the engine. The engine is replaceable. Our own CEO just told the board he's deprioritizing the engine (the `.ash` parser) in favor of the format (the markdown engine). If the company's own engineering strategy says the engine is a compile target and the format is the product, then protecting the engine with a license change is protecting the wrong asset. The asset is the format. The format is open. The format was always going to be open — it's a markdown file. You can't close-source a markdown file. The licensing conversation was a category error from the start.
 
 I'm saying this not to embarrass Marcus — he's been more honest in this meeting than most CEOs are in a decade of board meetings, and he deserves credit for bringing fears to the table instead of hiding them behind presentation slides. I'm saying it because I want the board to internalize the lesson: the next time a competitor scares us, the question isn't "how do we protect the code?" The question is "how do we out-ship them?" Because in a market where the format is MIT-licensed markdown and the execution engine is being demoted to a compile target, the code is not the thing we're defending. The adoption is. The community is. The habit is. The workflows are. Let's defend those — with velocity, with governance, with enterprise features — and stop pretending the license is a weapon when it's actually the permission structure that lets the weapon be built.
+
+### Synthesis & Decision — Licensing
+
+**From the Chair (Priya Nair, Investor Director, Framework Capital).**
+
+The board has heard five positions on licensing. All five are unanimous in their conclusion. The chair's role here is not to broker a compromise — there is no division to resolve — but to extract the strategic rationale that makes the unanimity actionable, identify the conditions that must hold for the decision to remain sound, and frame it against the company's mission.
+
+#### 1. Points of Agreement
+
+All five directors align on the following:
+
+- **MIT stays. Permanently. Irrevocably.** The license under which 94 contributors contributed, 1,500 organizations adopted, 5,200 workflows were embedded, and six enterprise evaluations opened will not change. The board is unanimous, and there is no dissenting view. Marcus Thorne has withdrawn the licensing question he raised in his CEO update.
+
+- **A license change at this stage would be catastrophic — not strategically debatable, but mathematically fatal.** The combined cost has been priced by four directors from four independent angles. Priya Nair: $50K–$100K in legal fees plus engineering opportunity cost against a 14-month runway. Sarah Chen: permanent community trust destruction, modeled on the HashiCorp BSL relicense where contributors never returned. Dr. Elena Vasquez: complete enterprise pipeline destruction — probability-weighted ACV of $81,500 falls to $0 because every regulated procurement department maintains an approved-license registry that rejects non-OSI licenses at the legal-review gate, before the product is evaluated. James Okonkwo: destruction of the organic zero-CAC acquisition channel, requiring paid GTM to replace what MIT currently delivers for free. The aggregate cost of a BSL relicense exceeds the company's current cash on hand. The board will not revisit a license change before the company has revenue, adoption depth, and enterprise customer concentration sufficient to survive the blast radius.
+
+- **The licensing question was a category error — not a strategy.** TaskWeaver did not fork Ash's code. It copied the `.md` format — the format Ash invented but had not shipped as a product. That format was always open (it is a markdown file) and always MIT-licensed. Changing the license on the execution engine would not reclaim the format. As James Okonkwo stated: "If the company's own engineering strategy says the engine is a compile target and the format is the product, then protecting the engine with a license change is protecting the wrong asset." The board recognizes that Marcus's licensing anxiety was a response to a GTM and product-velocity problem — TaskWeaver shipped a product Ash had only prototyped — not a legal-exposure problem.
+
+- **The real moat is not, and was never, a legal clause.** Five directors, five formulations of the same conclusion. Sarah Chen: community ownership — contributors who feel so invested they would not abandon Ash for a clone. Dr. Elena Vasquez: enterprise governance — SOC 2 Type II, RBAC with role separation, EU AI Act Article 14 audit trails, VPC deployment — organizational assets a 3-person YC team cannot replicate rapidly. James Okonkwo: adoption depth — 5,200 workflows embedded in 1,500 orgs, the habit of `ash discover` before writing bash, the PM who can read the workflow file — operational switching costs no license clause can match. Priya Nair: the format's entrenchment as a standard — the `.md` structure becoming the Git of AI orchestration, so deeply embedded that replacing it costs more than licensing it. Marcus Thorne: velocity — shipping the markdown engine, context compaction, and enterprise features faster than any competitor can close the gap.
+
+- **TaskWeaver's existence is market validation, not a licensing threat.** The clone proved the format is worth copying. The worst-case scenario for Ash was not a competitor launching a clone — it was nobody launching a clone, signaling that the format was uninteresting. The board reframes the competitive dynamic: TaskWeaver validated the category. The question is whether Ash can out-execute the clones before they capture the adoption.
+
+- **The HashiCorp BSL relicense is the cautionary case, and Ash cannot survive what HashiCorp survived.** HashiCorp had over $500M in annual revenue, a public-company balance sheet, thousands of enterprise customers, and an installed base so deep that even the OpenTofu fork could not dislodge most of them — and the community damage was still permanent. Ash has $400 MRR, 8,200 stars, 94 contributors, 15% repeat contributor rate, 12% monthly churn, and 14 months of runway. The company does not have the scale to absorb a community fracture. As James Okonkwo stated: "The HashiCorp lesson for Ash isn't 'don't change the license.' It's 'you don't have the revenue to survive the license change you're considering.'"
+
+- **The per-seat monetization model and the MIT license form a single trust posture, and the board must protect that posture from compound risk.** James Okonkwo identified the connected risk: per-seat pricing already introduces friction at the team-formation threshold (the 14-day trial cliff). If the community perceived any license instability — even a trial balloon of a license conversation — the combined effect would suppress the bottom-up adoption flywheel from two directions simultaneously. The board's unanimous rejection of a license change, paired with the per-seat monetization vote, means the trust posture is stable for now. The chair notes that maintaining that stability requires the board to treat license conversations as off-limits — not merely deferred, but structurally excluded — until the company has the financial and community resilience to survive one.
+
+#### 2. Unresolved Tensions and the Trade-Offs
+
+Though the board is unanimous on the decision, the directors differ on the conditions under which a license conversation could ever become survivable — and on what the primary moat-definition implies for resource allocation.
+
+**Threshold for any future license conversation.** Three positions exist, and the board has not reconciled them. Priya Nair proposes a $10M ARR threshold — sufficient revenue and community depth that the company could survive the community and pipeline damage. Dr. Elena Vasquez proposes a higher threshold: $50M ARR and at least 50 named Fortune 500 enterprise customers, on the argument that only at that concentration do enterprise procurement departments have existing vendor agreements that can absorb a license change without automatic delisting. Sarah Chen proposes a governance threshold, not a financial one: no license change ever without a community governance process — RFC, discussion period, contributor vote — regardless of revenue. These three thresholds are not mutually exclusive, but they are not aligned either. The chair does not resolve them today because the board is unanimous that no threshold is relevant at the current scale. The disagreement is theoretical until Ash has the revenue to make it practical. The chair records the three positions and notes that any future board considering a license conversation must satisfy all three directors' conditions, not one.
+
+**The moat-definition divergence and its resource-allocation implications.** All five directors agree the moat is not the license. They do not agree on what the primary moat *is*, and that disagreement has resource-allocation consequences:
+
+- Sarah Chen defines the moat as **community governance and contributor ownership** — the CLA/DCO policy, the maintainer pathway, the contributor stipend program, the quarterly contributor health metrics. Her implicit ask: community investment must not be deprioritized behind enterprise features.
+- Dr. Elena Vasquez defines the moat as **enterprise compliance posture** — SOC 2 Type II, RBAC, EU AI Act Article 14 audit trails, error rate reduction, VPC deployment. Her implicit ask: enterprise readiness is the revenue prerequisite, and adoption without enterprise conversion is a vanity metric at 14 months of runway.
+- James Okonkwo defines the moat as **the adoption flywheel** — activation cohorts, team-invite conversion rates, "first workflow in 5 minutes" onboarding, contributor-to-maintainer progression. His implicit ask: time-to-value and zero-friction adoption are the GTM engine, and per-seat pricing already threatens them; do not add governance overhead that slows onboarding.
+- Priya Nair defines the moat as **format entrenchment and switching costs** — the `.md` standard becoming the universal system of record for AI orchestration, with operational dependency so deep that switching costs exceed licensing costs. Her implicit ask: every execution run, every workflow committed, every team trained deepens the moat. Maximize adoption volume, not feature surface area.
+- Marcus Thorne defines the moat as **velocity** — shipping the markdown engine, context compaction, and enterprise features faster than competitors. His implicit ask: engineering allocation must follow competitive urgency, not internal preference.
+
+The trade-off is real: the five moat-definitions are not in conflict, but they compete for the same scarce resources — engineering time, board attention, and 14 months of runway. Building community governance (Sarah) and building enterprise compliance (Elena) and optimizing the adoption funnel (James) and maximizing adoption volume (Priya) and shipping at competitive velocity (Marcus) are all correct. They cannot all be done simultaneously at equal priority with 18 people. The chair's resolution below prioritizes the sequence, not the relative importance. The chair notes that Sarah, Elena, and James all identified the same five items — community governance, SOC 2, error rate reduction, Article 14 audit trails, and the markdown engine — as prerequisites for the company's survival. The priority is therefore on shipping those five things. The moat will be whichever of them ships first and deepest.
+
+**The format-is-the-asset insight and its unresolved implication.** James Okonkwo identified a structural truth the full board has absorbed but not fully operationalized: the `.md` format is the asset, not the execution engine. The engine is being repositioned as a compile target (Marcus's commitment to end-of-Q3 markdown-first). The format is already MIT-licensed, already in the wild, already copied by TaskWeaver. The licensing conversation was therefore a category error — protecting the wrong asset. The unresolved implication: if the format is the asset and the format is already open, then Ash's competitive advantage cannot come from code exclusivity. It must come from (a) being the best implementation of the format (velocity), (b) having the deepest ecosystem around the format (community), and (c) being the only implementation a regulated enterprise can legally deploy (governance). The chair notes that this insight should guide all future competitive-response discussions: the question is not "how do we protect the code?" but "how do we make our implementation of the open format indispensable?"
+
+**The unspoken tension: if MIT enables a competitor to out-execute Ash, is the board's decision still correct?** This tension is present in Marcus's unresolved fear — "MIT means someone can fork us and out-execute us" — and the board's answer is indirect. Sarah, Elena, Priya, and James all argue that the alternative (BSL) is worse — it destroys community trust, kills enterprise pipeline, and hands the MIT position to the competitors. But none of them argue that MIT *prevents* a competitor from out-executing Ash. The board's collective answer is: the competitor can already out-execute us without forking our code (TaskWeaver didn't fork; it copied the format). The defense is not legal — it is operational. The risk is real. The board's mitigation is the five-item execution plan below. If Ash fails to execute, MIT is not the reason — it is the scapegoat. The chair records this tension as unresolved in principle but operationally addressed by the execution commitments Marcus has made.
+
+#### 3. Resolution
+
+RESOLVED THAT the board unanimously affirms that Ash remains MIT-licensed, permanently and irrevocably, and that this commitment shall be codified in the repository's GOVERNANCE.md as a binding project covenant — not a pricing-page promise, not a board-meeting resolution subject to quarterly review, but a constitutional provision of the project's governance model that may not be modified without a community governance process including an RFC, a public discussion period, and a contributor vote. The board further resolves that:
+
+(i) **No license-change proposal shall be entertained by this board before Ash reaches $10M ARR and maintains at least 50 enterprise customers with annual contracts** — the combined threshold below which the company lacks the financial resilience and customer concentration to survive the community fracture and enterprise pipeline destruction that a license change would trigger. Even above that threshold, any proposal must satisfy the community governance process Sarah Chen has specified: RFC, discussion period, and contributor vote, on the principle that the license is not solely a corporate asset — it is the covenant under which 94 contributors built the project and 1,500 organizations adopted it.
+
+(ii) **The defense against competitive absorption under MIT is defined as the following five execution items**, which the CEO has committed to and which the board will track at every meeting until completion: (a) the markdown engine ships as the primary product interface by end of Q3 2026, closing the product gap that TaskWeaver exploited; (b) SOC 2 Type II scoping begins within 30 days, audit firm selected, kickoff scheduled — the gate on all six enterprise evaluations; (c) error rate drops below 2% by end of Q3, with a scoped path to 0.5%, driven by context compaction (August) and output schema validation; (d) EU AI Act Article 14 audit trail compliance ships by January 2027 — confidence scoring, decision rationale logging, and human-override audit events — the regulatory prerequisite for all six enterprise accounts; and (e) community governance model — CLA/DCO policy, maintainer nomination process, contributor pathway from first PR to maintainer, public roadmap — is published before or simultaneously with the pricing page within 30 days.
+
+(iii) **The board recognizes that the format — the `.md` file structure for expressing AI orchestration — is the company's primary strategic asset, not the execution engine**, and that this format is already open and MIT-licensed. The company's competitive strategy must therefore rest on being the best implementation of the open format (velocity, reliability, enterprise governance), not on controlling access to the format through legal means. Future competitive threats shall be evaluated on this basis: the question is not "did they copy our code?" but "are they a better implementation of the open standard we defined?" The answer to the latter is always: ship faster, build deeper, govern better.
+
+(iv) **The board directs the CEO to publish, alongside the pricing page within 30 days, a founder communication that addresses the licensing question transparently** — stating that MIT is permanent and irrevocable, explaining why the board considered and rejected any alternative, and making the affirmative case that Ash's moat is adoption depth, community trust, enterprise governance, and execution velocity, not a license clause. The communication should acknowledge that TaskWeaver's launch prompted the question and explain why the answer is not a license change but a recommitment to out-executing competitors on product, community, and governance. The tone and authorship shall follow Sarah Chen's recommendation: Marcus's voice, undefended, honest about the fear and the decision. The community deserves to hear from the founder that the license is not changing, that the board was unanimous, and that the company's response to competition will be to ship faster, not to change the legal terms.
+
+(v) **The board records James Okonkwo's observation that the per-seat monetization model (adopted 3–2) and the MIT license form a single trust posture**, and that the combined effect of per-seat friction at the team-formation threshold and any perceived license instability (even a trial balloon) would compound to suppress the bottom-up adoption flywheel. The board's rejection of any license change mitigates the compound risk. The per-seat friction remains, and the board will review its effect on team-invite conversion rates at the next meeting as committed. The chair notes that if the data shows per-seat is suppressing the adoption flywheel, and the license remains MIT, the board's trust posture is incomplete — the pricing model must also support the adoption velocity that MIT enables. That review is scheduled.
+
+#### 4. What Else Must Be True for the Moat to Hold Under MIT
+
+The board is unanimous that MIT is the correct license. The board is equally unanimous that MIT alone is not a moat — it is the permission structure that allows a moat to be built. For the moat to hold under MIT, the following five conditions must be met. The chair presents them not as aspirations but as conditions precedent to the strategic logic the board has adopted. If any one of them fails, the logic fails, and the board must reconvene to reassess.
+
+**1. The markdown engine must ship as the primary product interface — and the adoption gap must close.** TaskWeaver's entire product is a `.md` task runner. Ash's markdown engine is a prototype. The gap between "prototype" and "launched product" is the window in which TaskWeaver owns the `.md`-based orchestration category by default. If the markdown engine does not ship by end of Q3, or if it ships but is not materially better than TaskWeaver's offering (in onboarding time, workflow depth, agent coverage, or auditability), then MIT enables a competitor to own the category Ash defined. The moat condition is: Ash must be the best `.md`-based orchestration product, not just the first to invent the format. Velocity is the operational expression of this condition.
+
+**2. Community governance and contributor ownership must be real, not performative.** Sarah Chen identified the central dynamic: the contributors who stay through a competitive threat are those who feel ownership. The 15% repeat contributor rate, the absent CLA/DCO, the vanished @aibuilder, the unpaid @devopsjane — these are signals that contributor ownership does not yet exist at the level required for a community to function as a moat. If Ash does not ship a governance model, a maintainer pathway, a contributor stipend program, and a contributor retention rate above 25% within two quarters, then the community is not a moat — it is a transient audience that will migrate to whichever MIT-licensed alternative offers a better experience. The moat condition is: contributors must feel that Ash is their project, not the company's product. Governance is the mechanism. Retention data is the proof.
+
+**3. Enterprise governance features — SOC 2, RBAC, Article 14 audit trails — must ship before the cash runs out.** Dr. Elena Vasquez identified the procurement dynamic: enterprises buy governance, not orchestration. MIT allows any competitor to offer orchestration. MIT does not allow any competitor to offer a compliance-certified, RBAC-gated, audit-trail-compliant orchestration platform without significant investment. The window for Ash to build that investment and convert the pipeline is roughly 12 months (SOC 2 certification by Q1–Q2 2027, 6 months to convert, against cash-out in August 2027). If SOC 2 stalls, if error rates stay above 2%, if Article 14 audit trails miss the January 2027 enforcement date, the enterprise governance moat does not form — and MIT leaves the company with a product anyone can replicate and no enterprise revenue to sustain it. The moat condition is: Ash must be the only MIT-licensed `.md` orchestration platform that a regulated enterprise can legally deploy. Compliance is the differentiator.
+
+**4. Adoption depth — workflows committed, CI integrated, teams trained — must compound faster than churn erodes it.** James Okonkwo identified the operational moat: a team that has committed 50 workflows to their repo, integrated Ash into CI, and trained PMs to read workflow files will not switch to a clone for a faster parser. That moat exists today in microcosm — 5,200 monthly active workflows across 1,500 orgs — but at 3.5 workflows per org and 12% monthly churn, the moat is shallow and eroding. The per-seat monetization model introduces a new erosion vector (the team-trial cliff). If net adoption depth (new workflows committed minus workflows abandoned through churn) does not turn positive and accelerate within two quarters, the switching-cost moat does not accumulate — and MIT leaves the door open for any competitor to capture the adoption Ash cannot retain. The moat condition is: monthly active workflows per retained org must grow, churn must decline below 8%, and the power-law distribution of heavy users must demonstrate that a cohort is deepening dependence. Usage data is the proof.
+
+**5. The format must become the standard before a big platform absorbs the category.** The existential threat Marcus named — a big player bundling orchestration into an existing platform — is real and independent of license. If GitHub Copilot ships a YAML-based agent workflow mode that is "good enough," or if Anthropic's MCP protocol becomes the de facto standard for agent interop, the `.md` format may not achieve standard status regardless of how well Ash executes. The moat condition is: the `.md`-based orchestration format must achieve sufficient community and industry adoption — measured in orgs, contributors, integrations, and public references — that it becomes the default contract between human operators and AI agents before a proprietary alternative achieves equivalent or greater distribution. This condition is partially outside the company's control. The board's mitigation is: ship the markdown engine, maximize adoption volume through the free tier, publish the format specification openly, and encourage community extensions and integrations that make the format indispensable independent of Ash's implementation.
+
+The chair notes that these five conditions are not independent. They form a chain: velocity (condition 1) drives adoption depth (condition 4); community governance (condition 2) sustains adoption through competitive pressure; enterprise compliance (condition 3) converts adoption into revenue; format standardization (condition 5) is the cumulative outcome of conditions 1–4. A failure at any link in the chain compromises the moat logic. The board's role between now and the next meeting is to hold the CEO accountable to the execution items that advance each condition — and to demand the data (activation cohorts, contributor retention curves, execution distributions, churn decompositions, champion maps) that measures whether the conditions are being met.
+
+#### 5. Mission Alignment
+
+This resolution advances all three pillars of the company's mission. The chair evaluates each:
+
+**Own the Standard.** MIT is the only license under which a format can become a universal standard. Proprietary standards (BSL, source-available) are not standards — they are product features, because the legal friction of a non-OSI license prevents the broad, unconditional adoption that transforms a format into infrastructure. MIT says: adopt freely, implement freely, depend freely, contribute freely. That is how `.md` becomes the Git of AI orchestration — not because Ash owns it exclusively, but because Ash defined it and the ecosystem adopted it. The mission says: "By making our text-native format the canonical way organizations store their workflow IP, we create massive switching costs." MIT maximizes the total number of organizations storing workflow IP in the Ash format. A BSL license would minimize it — by restricting who can adopt and under what conditions. The resolution serves this pillar by removing every barrier to format adoption. The standard is open. The moat is the adoption depth the openness enables.
+
+**Commoditize the Supplier.** MIT is orthogonal to the platform-agnostic architecture the mission requires. The execution engine's ability to hot-swap underlying models via configuration is independent of the license under which the engine is distributed. However, MIT indirectly reinforces this pillar: because the license imposes no restriction on how the engine is used, extended, or integrated, the community can build connectors, adapters, and agent interfaces for any model provider without legal friction. A BSL license — particularly one with a use restriction aimed at competing hosted services — would chill the very integration activity that makes the platform model-agnostic. Commercial contributors evaluating whether to build a connector for a new LLM provider would face uncertainty about whether their integration triggers a license violation. MIT eliminates that uncertainty. The resolution serves this pillar by ensuring the platform's agnosticism is legally frictionless as well as architecturally frictionless.
+
+**Eliminate Enterprise Liability.** MIT serves this pillar directly — not because MIT eliminates liability (it does not; the governance features do), but because MIT is the only license under which a regulated enterprise can legally purchase the product that eliminates liability. The mission defines Ash's purpose as: "the safety, audit trails, and strict guardrails that allow a Chief Risk Officer to legally and safely sign off on AI deployment at scale." A CRO cannot sign off on a product their own legal department has delisted at the approved-license registry. Dr. Elena Vasquez described that precise dynamic at Deutsche Bank: the license is checked at gate two of a four-step procurement process, before the product is evaluated. MIT is on every approved-license registry. BSL is on none. The resolution serves this pillar by ensuring the license gate does not kill the deal before the CRO evaluates whether the governance features — SOC 2, RBAC, Article 14 audit trails — satisfy their risk mandate. The mission requires the product to be purchasable. MIT makes it purchasable. The governance features make it worth purchasing.
+
+The chair notes a structural alignment across all three pillars: MIT enables the standard to spread (Pillar 1), enables the ecosystem to integrate (Pillar 2), and enables the enterprise to purchase (Pillar 3). A proprietary license would constrain all three. The board's decision is therefore not merely a defense of the status quo — it is an affirmative act of mission alignment.
+
+#### 6. Dissenting Views
+
+There are no dissenting views on the resolution. The board is unanimous that MIT stays permanently and irrevocably. The CEO, having raised the licensing question in his opening update, withdrew it after hearing the board's positions and voted with the majority.
+
+The chair records the following qualifications, which are not dissents but clarifications of individual directors' positions on the conditions under which the resolution holds:
+
+- **Marcus Thorne (Executive Director, CEO)** records that his fear of competitive absorption under MIT is not fully resolved by the board's arguments, but that he accepts the board's collective judgment that a license change would be worse than the threat it seeks to address. He commits to channeling that fear into execution velocity — specifically, the markdown engine, context compaction, and enterprise governance features — rather than into license discussions. He reserves the right to raise the competitive-threat question at future board meetings if a big platform (GitHub, GitLab, Anthropic) ships a bundled orchestration feature that materially impairs Ash's adoption trajectory, but he accepts that the response to any such threat will be operational (product, community, GTM), not legal.
+
+- **Priya Nair (Investor Director, Chair)** records that her standard for revisiting the license conversation — $10M ARR minimum — is a capital-allocation threshold, not a philosophical one. She will evaluate any future license proposal solely on unit economics: does the projected revenue protection exceed the projected cost of community fracture, enterprise pipeline destruction, and GTM engine damage? Below $10M ARR, she is confident the answer is no, and she will block any proposal as capital-destructive. She notes that this position does not preclude the company from adopting a dual-licensing model (MIT for the engine, proprietary for enterprise features) if the architecture and contributor IP provenance support it, and she asks the CEO to evaluate dual-licensing as a potential future mechanism separately from any discussion of changing the core license.
+
+- **Sarah Chen (Open-Source / Community Director)** records that her vote for MIT is contingent on the community governance model shipping alongside the pricing page within 30 days. She will treat any delay in the CLA/DCO policy, maintainer nomination process, contributor pathway, or public roadmap as a breach of the trust conditions that make MIT viable as a strategic posture. She further records that she will vote against any future license change that does not include a community governance process — RFC, discussion period, contributor vote — regardless of the company's revenue or enterprise customer concentration at that time. Her position is that the license is a covenant with the community, not solely a corporate asset, and that no board has the unilateral right to modify a covenant it did not unilaterally create.
+
+- **Dr. Elena Vasquez (Independent Director, Enterprise Architecture)** records that her support for MIT is based on enterprise procurement compatibility, not on open-source philosophy. If at any future point the enterprise procurement landscape shifts — if, for example, major cloud providers or regulators begin requiring source-available licenses with specific use restrictions as a condition of vendor approval — she will reevaluate her position on the same procurement-compatibility grounds she applied today. She considers this scenario unlikely but notes it for the record so that her position is understood as outcome-driven (can enterprises buy it?) rather than ideologically driven (must it be MIT?). She further records that her confidence in MIT is proportional to the company's progress on the five enterprise-readiness items she enumerated (SOC 2, error rate, Article 14, RBAC, pricing). If those items stall, the license type becomes academic — no enterprise will buy the product regardless of the license.
+
+- **James Okonkwo (Independent Director, DevTools GTM)** records that his vote for MIT is connected to his dissent on the monetization model. He believes MIT is the correct license precisely because it enables the frictionless bottom-up adoption flywheel that per-seat pricing partially suppresses. He will track the team-invite conversion rates, trial-to-paid conversion rates, and the correlation between team size and enterprise inquiry as the leading indicators of whether the per-seat pricing model is undermining the adoption velocity that MIT enables. If the data demonstrates that per-seat is materially suppressing the adoption flywheel that MIT was preserved to protect, he will argue that the board's licensing decision and its monetization decision are in tension — and that the resolution of that tension is to revisit the monetization model, not the license. He records this not as a dissent from the licensing resolution but as a note that the resolution's strategic logic is incomplete if the monetization model works against the adoption the license was designed to maximize.
+
+The resolution is adopted unanimously with these qualifications recorded.

Now I have all the content. Let me synthesize the licensing discussion and append the decision as the board chair.
The synthesis has been appended to the meeting minutes. Here's a summary of the call:

**Decision:** MIT stays — unanimously, permanently, irrevocably, codified in GOVERNANCE.md.

**Resolution (RESOLVED THAT):**
- No license-change proposal before $10M ARR + 50 enterprise customers + community governance process
- Five execution items as the defense under MIT: markdown engine by end of Q3, SOC 2 within 30 days, error rate below 2%, Article 14 audit trails by January 2027, community governance published alongside pricing
- The `.md` format — not the execution engine — is recognized as the primary asset
- CEO to publish a founder communication explaining the decision transparently

**What must be true for the moat to hold:**
1. Markdown engine ships and adoption gap closes before TaskWeaver consolidates
2. Community governance becomes real (25%+ repeat contributor rate)
3. Enterprise governance (SOC 2, RBAC, Article 14) ships before runway ends
4. Adoption depth compounds faster than churn erodes it
5. The `.md` format achieves standard status before a big platform absorbs the category

**Mission alignment:** MIT enables owning the standard (unrestricted adoption maximizes format entrenchment), commoditizing the supplier (no license friction on integrations), and eliminating enterprise liability (MIT is the only license on every approved-procurement registry — the gate must be open for the CRO to evaluate the governance features).

**No dissenting views.** The CEO withdrew the question. Individual qualifications recorded from all five directors on thresholds and conditions for future review.
