+---
+
+## 5. Functional Deep Dive — DSL vs Markdown Engine
+
+### Position: Marcus Thorne (Executive Director, Co-Founder & CEO)
+
+This is the hardest thing I've been asked to do in this meeting. Harder than admitting I can't give Priya a credible revenue forecast. Harder than pulling the AE job description after James told me I'd be setting someone up to fail. Harder than conceding to Elena that I wouldn't sign off on Ash at Deutsche Bank. Because this isn't about a number I don't have or a hire I shouldn't make. This is about something I built with my own hands — and whether I should unbuild it.
+
+I wrote the `.ash` parser in a weekend. That's not a brag. That's a diagnosis. I'd spent six months at Shopify watching three platform teams run incompatible shell scripts to orchestrate the same set of coding agents, and in one weekend I wrote something that made all three scripts unnecessary. It parsed. It dispatched. It logged. It worked. And in that weekend, I felt something I hadn't felt in years as a staff platform engineer: the conviction that I'd seen a problem nobody else had seen clearly enough to solve, and I'd solved it. That weekend was the reason Ash exists. The parser is the ur-commit. It's the thing I built before I had investors, before I had a team, before I had a board — back when this was just me, a problem, and a weekend.
+
+So when Priya asks whether I'm prepared to sunset it, and when Sarah says the community is voting toward markdown, and when Elena says the DSL is a procurement liability, and when James says we lost a deal to bash because of the thing I built — I want this board to understand that what you're asking me to do is not a resource-allocation decision. It's not a GTM pivot. It's not a product deprecation. It's asking me to look at the thing that made me start this company and say: it was the right idea, in the wrong form, for the wrong stage.
+
+And I'm going to do that. Not because the board convinced me — I knew this intellectually before I walked into the room. I said as much in my CEO update. The markdown engine covers 80% of use cases with zero learning curve. I said I'd rather sunset something else. I said I needed the board to help me separate ego from engineering reality. You've done that. Now let me tell you what it feels like from my side, because this board has asked for honesty, and this is the part of the meeting where I have to be more honest than I've been about anything else today.
+
+**What the DSL actually is — not what I feel about it, but what it does.**
+
+The `.ash` expression language is not a general-purpose programming language. It's a domain-specific syntax for expressing agent orchestration — which agent to call, with which prompt, under which conditions, with what branching logic, and with what error handling. The parser tokenizes those expressions into an execution graph. The engine walks the graph, dispatches to agents, collects outputs, evaluates conditions, and branches. The DSL is the interface between the human intent and the machine execution.
+
+The language was designed for three things:
+
+First, **expressiveness.** A `.ash` file can express parallel agent dispatch, conditional branching on agent output, retry-with-fallback chains, and state-passing between steps — things that are syntactically awkward or impossible in a flat markdown table. A five-agent workflow where two run in parallel, results are merged, a third evaluates the merged output against a threshold, and the fourth and fifth branch on that evaluation — that's four lines of `.ash` syntax. In markdown, it's a deeply nested table with cross-references that a PM cannot follow and a developer cannot debug. The DSL's expressiveness is real. It's not aesthetic. It's functional.
+
+Second, **determinism.** The parser is a deterministic state machine. Given the same `.ash` file, it produces the same execution graph, every time. The engine can validate the graph before execution — detecting cycles, missing agent references, unreachable branches — and reject malformed workflows at parse time, not at step 27. The markdown engine, as currently prototyped, parses tables into a graph but has no static analysis capability. A malformed markdown workflow doesn't fail until the engine tries to execute step 27 and discovers the agent reference was misspelled. The DSL catches that at parse time. For Elena's enterprise use case — deterministic execution with predictable failure modes — the DSL is technically superior to the markdown prototype in its current form. That's not ego. That's engineering.
+
+Third, **compactness.** A 50-step workflow in `.ash` fits on one screen. In markdown with the table format, a 50-step workflow is a document the length of a legal brief. The compactness matters for two reasons: (a) the context window — a 50-step workflow in compact DSL syntax consumes fewer tokens than an equivalent markdown table, which means the engine has more headroom before hitting the context limit, and (b) developer ergonomics — a workflow you can see on one screen is a workflow you can reason about. Elena's point about silent degradation at the context boundary is real, and the DSL's compactness was designed specifically to push that boundary further out.
+
+Those three properties — expressiveness, determinism, and compactness — are not trivial. They are the engineering rationale for why the DSL exists. They are why I've spent 60% of engineering time on the parser and execution engine. And they are why, when someone says "just use markdown," my engineer brain pushes back: markdown doesn't do these things. Not yet. Not at the scale and reliability the engine currently provides.
+
+But here's what the DSL does not do — and this is the part I've been avoiding.
+
+**What the DSL costs — the ledger I should have kept and didn't.**
+
+One: **adoption friction.** The platform engineer who told us "I don't know if I want to bet on a new language when bash already works" was not wrong. He was telling us something I didn't want to hear: the DSL is a gate. It is a thing a developer must learn before Ash provides value. `ash discover` auto-detects workflows — but `ash discover` detects `.ash` files and `.md` files. If the first thing a developer sees is a custom syntax they have to learn, and the alternative is a bash script they already know, the evaluation is not about features. It's about learning cost. The bash-script loss wasn't a GTM failure, as James framed it. It was a product-design failure. We asked a developer to pay a learning tax before he'd received any value. He declined. Any user who declines because of the learning tax is a user who never reaches the features that make Ash valuable — the cross-agent orchestration, the audit trails, the deterministic rollback. The DSL is supposed to be the interface to those features. Instead, for a meaningful percentage of potential users, it's the barrier that prevents them from ever seeing those features.
+
+Two: **community contribution surface.** Sarah is right. I don't have the PR breakdown by format — `.ash` versus `.md` — and the fact that I don't have it tells me I've been avoiding the answer. But I know anecdotally what the community is telling us. The top-voted issue is #198 — parallel execution within `.md` files — with 32 thumbs-up. The customer quote I keep citing in this meeting is about `.md` files. The community discussions are about markdown. The contributors who answer forum questions and write tutorials are explaining `.md` file structure, not `.ash` syntax. PMs, designers, and non-engineering stakeholders — exactly the constituency the mission document says is critical for standard entrenchment — cannot read a `.ash` file. They can read a markdown table. The DSL limits the contributor surface to engineers who are willing to learn a new syntax. The markdown engine expands it to anyone who can read a table. At 94 contributors and 15% repeat rate, we cannot afford to limit the contributor surface. We need it to be as wide as possible. The DSL narrows it. Markdown widens it. That's not a design preference. That's math.
+
+Three: **enterprise procurement risk.** Elena named something I hadn't internalized: a custom programming language — even a domain-specific one — is a procurement rejection criterion in regulated environments. The three questions she posed are the exact questions a bank's architecture team will ask: "Who maintains this language? Who trains our engineers? What happens if your company disappears — who can read our 200 `.ash` files?" I don't have answers that satisfy a vendor risk assessment. The markdown engine answers all three: domain experts can read it, existing engineers can maintain it, the format survives the vendor relationship. The DSL is a procurement liability not because it's a bad language, but because it's an *additional* language — and regulated enterprises have policies against introducing new languages into a production stack without an approved maintainer, a training plan, and a continuity guarantee. I didn't know that was a procurement criterion when I wrote the parser. I do now.
+
+Four: **key-person risk, made structural.** I'm the only person who deeply understands the parser and the execution engine. I acknowledged that in my CEO update. Priya reframed it as a resource-allocation question: maintaining two formats with a single point of failure is a self-inflicted wound. She's right. If the DSL becomes a compile target — something the engine generates internally from markdown input — the key-person risk localizes to the compiler, not the user interface. Engineers can contribute to the markdown parsing layer without understanding the internal execution graph. Contributors can write workflow templates without learning the DSL syntax. The markdown engine doesn't eliminate the key-person risk — but it walls it off from the user-facing surface. That's the difference between "one person understands the product" and "one person understands one internal component." The first is a company-killing risk. The second is a staffing problem. Those are different categories of urgency.
+
+Five: **resource allocation — the 60/30/10 split.** This is the number that should embarrass me. Sixty percent of engineering time on the parser and execution engine. Thirty percent on Ash Cloud. Ten percent on the markdown prototype. The markdown engine covers 80% of use cases and gets 10% of resources. The `.ash` parser serves power-user expressiveness and gets 60%. That ratio is not just backwards — it's indefensible. If I were a board director evaluating this company instead of running it, I would ask the CEO the same question Priya asked: how are you going to maintain both with 14 months of runway and one person who understands half the codebase? The answer is: I'm not. I can't. The math doesn't work. The resource allocation has to invert. Markdown gets the majority of engineering resources — because markdown is the adoption surface, the community surface, the enterprise surface, and the competitive surface against TaskWeaver. The DSL gets whatever is left — and "whatever is left" at our team size and runway is "enough to keep it compiling, not enough to extend it."
+
+**What I'm keeping. What I'm letting go. And why.**
+
+I'm not ready to say "sunset the DSL." I said that in my CEO response to Priya, and I want to explain what I mean — because I think the word "sunset" has been thrown around this room in a way that conflates "what users see" with "what the engine does."
+
+The DSL is a real technical asset. The expressiveness — parallel dispatch, conditional branching, retry-with-fallback, state-passing — is not something I can replicate in a flat markdown table today. The determinism — parse-time validation, static graph analysis — is not something the markdown prototype has. The compactness — token efficiency for long workflows — is not something markdown provides. Those are real capabilities that a subset of our users — the power users running 50-step cross-agent workflows with complex branching logic — depend on. If I sunset the DSL entirely, I'm not removing a learning curve. I'm removing capabilities that don't yet have a markdown equivalent. That's not simplification. That's regression.
+
+What I'm committing to is this: the DSL becomes an internal compile target, not a user-facing interface. The markdown engine becomes the primary product surface — the thing new users see, the thing tutorials teach, the thing the community contributes to, the thing procurement evaluates. When a user writes a workflow in markdown, the engine parses the `.md` file, compiles it to an internal representation, and executes it. The internal representation can be the DSL — the thing the parser currently generates from `.ash` files. Or it can be a graph IR that both markdown and `.ash` compile to. I need to make that architectural decision this quarter, and I will.
+
+Under this model: new users write `.md` files. The engine accepts `.md` files. The documentation teaches `.md` files. The community contributes `.md` workflows. The markdown engine supports the basic capabilities — sequential step execution, agent dispatch, parallel execution where syntactically feasible, basic conditional branching — that cover 80% of use cases. The DSL continues to exist for power users — the 5% of users whose workflows require the expressiveness that markdown can't yet provide. Those users can write `.ash` files directly if they choose, and the engine will execute them. But `.ash` is not the default. It's not the tutorial. It's not the onboarding path. It's an advanced feature for advanced users, documented as such, maintained as such, and resourced as such — which means it gets advanced-feature engineering allocation, not 60%.
+
+The markdown engine ships as the primary product interface by end of Q3. The DSL becomes the advanced compile target. The resource split inverts: markdown engine and context compaction get the majority of engineering resources. The DSL parser gets maintenance and bug fixes — not new features, not syntax extensions, not the 60% allocation it has today. If the markdown engine, over time, evolves to support the expressiveness that currently requires the DSL — if a markdown extension or a structured YAML block embedded in `.md` files can express parallel dispatch, conditional branching, and retry-with-fallback — then the DSL's advanced use case shrinks to zero, and at that point, "sunset" becomes the honest word. But I'm not going to promise a sunset date for capabilities I haven't yet replicated in the markdown engine. I'm going to promise that the markdown engine is the priority, and the DSL's capabilities are the benchmark the markdown engine has to meet before the DSL can be retired.
+
+**Is the DSL a technical achievement or a distraction?**
+
+Both. And the "both" is what I've been struggling with.
+
+It's a technical achievement. It solved a real problem — three incompatible shell scripts across three platform teams at Shopify — with an elegant, deterministic, compact syntax that expressed agent orchestration better than anything available at the time. The parser is a well-designed state machine. The execution engine is a reliable graph walker. The error handling is deterministic. The static analysis catches malformed workflows at parse time. In a world where the alternative was bash scripts with no structure, no audit trail, and no cross-agent orchestration, the `.ash` DSL was a genuine advance. It's why Ash exists. It's why the first 4,100 GitHub stars happened before we had a markdown engine or an Ash Cloud or an `ash discover`. The DSL was the product — and for a certain kind of power user, it still is the best version of the product.
+
+It's also a distraction. The market doesn't want a new language. It wants a new format — a text-native, human-readable, version-controllable standard for expressing AI orchestration. It turns out the format is markdown. It doesn't need a custom syntax. It needs a structure — steps, agents, prompts, conditions — that fits into a format people already know. The DSL solved the structure problem by inventing a syntax. The markdown engine solves it by using a table. Tables are not as expressive as a custom syntax. But they are expressive enough for 80% of use cases, and they have zero learning curve, and PMs can read them, and procurement departments don't flag them, and competitors can't win by being "the open one" because the format is a markdown file — you can't close-source a table.
+
+The DSL was the right answer to the wrong question. The question I was asking in that weekend at Shopify was: "How do I express agent orchestration in the most powerful, compact, and deterministic way possible?" The question the market is asking is: "How do I express agent orchestration in the most accessible, auditable, and portable way possible?" Those are different questions. The DSL answers the first. Markdown answers the second. I built the company around the first question because I thought the power users — the platform engineers running 50-step cross-agent workflows — were the market. They're not. They're the power-user cohort. The market is the PM who needs to read the workflow. The platform engineer who needs to evaluate Ash against bash in five minutes. The procurement analyst who needs to know the format survives the vendor relationship. The contributor who wants to submit a PR without learning a new syntax. The community manager who wants to write a tutorial that a non-engineer can follow.
+
+The DSL is a technical achievement that became a distraction the moment the markdown engine proved that 80% of the value could be delivered with zero learning cost. I didn't see that because I was too close to the parser — too proud of the state machine, too invested in the expressiveness, too attached to the weekend that started the company. The board has given me the distance I couldn't give myself. Priya put the resource-allocation question in terms I couldn't deflect. Sarah put the community question in terms I couldn't ignore. Elena put the procurement question in terms I didn't know existed. James put the competitive question in terms that made the timeline real: TaskWeaver is shipping what we haven't shipped, and every week the markdown engine sits as a prototype is a week they own the `.md`-based orchestration category by default.
+
+The honest answer: the DSL is a distraction now. It wasn't always. In the first year of Ash, it was the product — the only product — and it was a good one. But the market has moved. The competitive landscape has formed. The community has spoken. The enterprise pipeline has requirements the DSL violates. Continuing to invest 60% of engineering time in the DSL at this stage, with this runway, against this competition, is not a commitment to technical excellence. It's an emotional attachment to the first thing I built, dressed up as a product strategy.
+
+**What I'm going to do about it.**
+
+I'm shipping the markdown engine as the primary product interface by end of Q3. I've committed to that date three times in this meeting — to Priya, to James, and now formally in this functional deep dive. September 30, 2026. The markdown engine is the first thing a new user sees. It's the documented interface. It's the tutorial path. It's the contribution surface.
+
+The DSL becomes an internal compile target — the representation the engine uses to execute workflows, whether those workflows were authored in markdown or in the DSL directly. New users never see the DSL. Power users can still write `.ash` files directly if their workflows require the expressiveness that markdown can't yet provide — but that path is documented as advanced usage, not primary onboarding. Engineering resources shift from 60% DSL / 10% markdown to roughly the inverse: markdown engine and context compaction get the majority, DSL gets maintenance.
+
+I commit to publishing the PR breakdown by format — `.ash` versus `.md` contributions — at the next board meeting. If the data shows what I expect — that the community is already voting for markdown with their pull requests — I will accelerate the transition and, if necessary, consider a sunset timeline for the DSL as a user-facing format. But I want the data before I make that call, because the power users who depend on the DSL's expressiveness deserve a migration path, not an announcement that their workflows no longer parse.
+
+I also commit to solving the key-person risk. The DSL compiler and execution engine need at least one other engineer who understands them deeply. I'm designating a senior engineer — likely the backend hire starting in August — to pair with me on the engine internals, with the explicit goal that by end of Q4 I am not the only person who can modify the execution graph or debug a parser failure. The DSL's internal role as a compile target reduces the urgency of this — because the user-facing surface (markdown) is more broadly understood — but it doesn't eliminate it. The engine is still the core technology. One person understanding it is still a company-killing risk. I'm fixing it.
+
+**Closing.**
+
+Priya, Sarah, Elena, James — you all asked me versions of the same question from different angles, and I gave you versions of the same answer in my CEO response. Let me say it more directly here.
+
+I wrote the `.ash` parser on a weekend because I was a platform engineer who saw a problem and knew how to solve it. That instinct — see a problem, solve it with code — is what made Ash exist. It's also what made me spend 60% of engineering time on a DSL while the markdown engine — the thing the market actually wanted — sat as a prototype. The same instinct that created the company nearly starved it of the product it needed to compete.
+
+The DSL isn't going away. It's going where it belongs — under the hood, powering an engine that users interact with through markdown. The expressiveness, determinism, and compactness I built into the parser aren't lost. They become internal properties of the engine. They're not features the user has to learn. They're capabilities the user gets for free — because the engine handles the complexity that the DSL was designed to express. That's the right architecture for this stage. That's the right allocation for this runway. That's the right answer to this board's questions.
+
+And personally: letting go of the DSL as the user-facing product is the hardest thing this board has asked me to do. Harder than pulling the AE hire. Harder than admitting I can't forecast revenue. Harder than conceding to Elena that I wouldn't sign off on Ash at Deutsche Bank. Because the DSL is the first thing I built, and the first thing you build is the thing you believe in most deeply — whether or not it's the thing the market wants. The board has helped me separate the belief from the evidence. I'm grateful for that — and I'm going to prove it by shipping the markdown engine, inverting the engineering allocation, and making the DSL a power tool instead of a front door. Key points covered:

- **The DSL's costs**: adoption friction (lost deal to bash), narrowed community contribution surface, enterprise procurement liability, structural key-person risk, and the indefensible 60/30/10 resource split

--- /opt/apps/agents/ash/workflows/board/board-output/2026-07-09/meeting-minutes.md
+++ /opt/apps/agents/ash/workflows/board/board-output/2026-07-09/meeting-minutes.md
@@ -1481,4 +1481,80 @@
 
 The DSL isn't going away. It's going where it belongs — under the hood, powering an engine that users interact with through markdown. The expressiveness, determinism, and compactness I built into the parser aren't lost. They become internal properties of the engine. They're not features the user has to learn. They're capabilities the user gets for free — because the engine handles the complexity that the DSL was designed to express. That's the right architecture for this stage. That's the right allocation for this runway. That's the right answer to this board's questions.
 
 And personally: letting go of the DSL as the user-facing product is the hardest thing this board has asked me to do. Harder than pulling the AE hire. Harder than admitting I can't forecast revenue. Harder than conceding to Elena that I wouldn't sign off on Ash at Deutsche Bank. Because the DSL is the first thing I built, and the first thing you build is the thing you believe in most deeply — whether or not it's the thing the market wants. The board has helped me separate the belief from the evidence. I'm grateful for that — and I'm going to prove it by shipping the markdown engine, inverting the engineering allocation, and making the DSL a power tool instead of a front door. The market deserves the front door. The power users get the power tool. The company gets the product it needs to survive. Thank you for pushing me to a decision I couldn't reach alone.
+
+### Position: Priya Nair (Investor Director, Framework Capital)
+
+Marcus, I'm going to start with appreciation, because this board asked you for honesty and you delivered more of it than I've heard in a boardroom in five years. Watching a founder look at the first thing they built and say "it was the right answer to the wrong question" is not something most CEOs can do. Most of them defend the ur-commit until the company runs out of money defending it. You didn't. That takes something, and I want it on the record that I see it.
+
+Now I'm going to do what I do. I'm going to put numbers on what you just said, because you made an emotional case for a resource-allocation decision, and emotional cases don't survive board reviews — spreadsheets do. Let's build the spreadsheet.
+
+**The 60/30/10 split — priced at our burn rate.**
+
+You told the board that engineering time is allocated roughly 60% to the `.ash` parser and execution engine, 30% to Ash Cloud, and 10% to the markdown prototype. Let's put dollar signs on that.
+
+Our monthly burn is $310,000. Personnel is $240,000 of that — roughly 77%. Engineering is 12 of 18 people — roughly 67% of headcount, or about $160,000/month in fully-loaded engineering cost. At 60% allocation to the DSL, that's $96,000 per month — over a million dollars a year — spent on a parser and execution engine that serves a format the company has just agreed to demote to a compile target. At 10% allocation to the markdown engine, that's $16,000 per month — under $200,000 a year — spent on the format that covers 80% of use cases, that the community is voting for with pull requests, that the enterprise pipeline requires, that TaskWeaver is already shipping, and that the board has unanimously identified as the primary product surface.
+
+That ratio is not a resource-allocation decision. It is a structural mispricing of the company's own strategy. And it has been mispriced for long enough that TaskWeaver — three people, two weeks, no burn rate to speak of — shipped a product we only had as a prototype. They didn't out-engineer us. They out-allocated us. They put 100% of their resources on the format the market wanted. We put 10% of ours on it and 60% on the one we were emotionally attached to. That's the spreadsheet. That's the story of the last quarter.
+
+**Cost per active workflow — the metric that should drive allocation.**
+
+My profile says I ask for the numbers. Here's the number I want: cost per active workflow, broken down by format. I don't have it yet — Marcus committed to the PR breakdown by format at the next meeting — but I can approximate it from the inputs, and the approximation is damning.
+
+5,200 monthly active workflows across 1,500 orgs. The DSL parser and execution engine consume roughly $96,000/month in engineering time. That's approximately $18.50 per active workflow per month just for the parser — before Ash Cloud infrastructure, before community support, before any other cost center. The markdown engine consumes roughly $16,000/month — about $3 per active workflow.
+
+But that's the wrong denominator. The DSL serves a subset of users — the power users who write `.ash` files directly. The markdown engine serves the entire user base — and, more importantly, the *potential* user base that hasn't adopted because of the DSL learning curve. If we assume, conservatively, that 80% of workflows could be expressed in markdown (your number, Marcus), and that the DSL is required for the remaining 20% (complex branching, parallel dispatch, retry chains), then the effective cost per DSL-requiring workflow is closer to $92 — five times the blended average. The markdown engine handles the other 80% of workflows for $16,000/month total — less than the cost of one fully-loaded engineer.
+
+Every dollar spent on the DSL parser beyond what's needed to keep it compiling is a dollar spent on a feature that serves, at most, 20% of workflows — at a per-unit cost that dwarfs the per-unit cost of the feature that serves 100% of the addressable market. That is not a resource-allocation argument. That is a capital-allocation argument. And at 14 months of runway, capital allocation arguments are the only ones that matter.
+
+**Responding to Marcus's technical case for the DSL.**
+
+Marcus, you made three engineering arguments for the DSL: expressiveness, determinism, and compactness. They're real. I'm not going to pretend they're not. But I want to price each one against the opportunity cost of the markdown engine delay.
+
+**Expressiveness.** Parallel dispatch, conditional branching, retry-with-fallback, state-passing. You said a five-agent workflow with parallel execution and branching is four lines of `.ash` syntax but a deeply nested markdown table. I believe you. But I also believe that the number of users whose workflows require that level of expressiveness — today, on this product, at this stage — is a rounding error against the number of users who just want to chain three agents sequentially and have it work. The bash-script loss wasn't about expressiveness. It was about a team running three agents. Three. Not fifty. Not parallel dispatch. Three sequential steps. The DSL's expressiveness advantage over markdown for a three-agent sequential workflow is zero. But the learning cost is non-zero. The evaluation cost is non-zero. The procurement risk is non-zero. We lost a deal to bash — bash, Marcus — because we showed a power-user syntax to a team that needed a sequential chain. The DSL's expressiveness is real. Its relevance to the adoption funnel is concentrated in a cohort so small that optimizing for it at 60% of engineering allocation is indefensible.
+
+**Determinism.** Parse-time validation, static graph analysis, rejection of malformed workflows before execution. You're right that the markdown prototype doesn't have this. But here's the thing: the markdown engine is a prototype that got 10% of engineering resources. It doesn't have static analysis because nobody built it. If the markdown engine had gotten the 60% allocation and the DSL had gotten 10%, the markdown engine would have static analysis and the DSL would be the one missing features. The determinism gap is not a property of the format — it's a property of the investment. Markdown tables can be validated at parse time. Misspelled agent references can be caught before step 27. The fact that the prototype doesn't do it is not an argument for the DSL. It's an argument for investing in the markdown engine — which is exactly the inversion you've committed to. I'm not worried about whether markdown can be deterministic. I'm worried about whether we'll invest enough in it fast enough to make it deterministic before TaskWeaver ships a deterministic markdown engine of their own.
+
+**Compactness.** A 50-step workflow in `.ash` fits on one screen. In markdown, it's a long document. Token efficiency matters for context windows. This is the strongest technical argument you made, and it's the one I'll grant you. But I want to put it in context. The top complaint in customer feedback is: "I tried to run a 50-step workflow and it got confused halfway through." That's the context window limit — the exact problem compactness was designed to solve. Context compaction is targeting August. After compaction ships, the reliable depth is roughly 100 steps. At 100 steps, the token-efficiency advantage of `.ash` over markdown starts to matter less — not zero, but less — because the engine has more headroom regardless of the input format. The DSL's compactness advantage was designed for a pre-compaction world. In a post-compaction world — where the engine can handle 100-step workflows in either format — the advantage shrinks from "existential" to "nice to have for power users." That's the right category for it. Not 60% of engineering.
+
+**The cost of delay — in dollars, in stars, in competitive positioning.**
+
+Every week the markdown engine sits as a prototype is a week TaskWeaver is the only `.md`-based orchestration tool shipping. They launched two weeks ago with 1,200 stars. At their trajectory, they pass 8,000 stars in roughly 14 weeks — two board meetings from now. They don't need to match our execution engine to capture the adoption base. They just need to be the first `.md`-based tool a developer tries — and right now, they are, because we haven't shipped ours.
+
+The markdown engine delay is not a product delay. It's a competitive-positioning delay measured in GitHub stars and developer mindshare. Every star TaskWeaver gains in the window between their launch and our markdown engine ship date is a star we have to win back — and winning back is more expensive than winning first. The cost of the DSL's 60% allocation is not just the $96,000/month in engineering time. It's the market position we're ceding to a 3-person YC team while we polish a parser.
+
+Put a number on it. If TaskWeaver captures 5,000 developers who would have adopted Ash — 5,000 stars, roughly 600 active users at a 12% activation rate, roughly 72 paying teams at a 12% team conversion rate — that's roughly $10,000/month in foregone team-tier revenue under the model this board just approved. Over 12 months, $120,000. That's the opportunity cost of every quarter the markdown engine is delayed. And it compounds — because the teams TaskWeaver captures today are the enterprise champions of 2028. We don't just lose the revenue. We lose the pipeline that the revenue was supposed to generate.
+
+**Is the DSL a technical achievement or a distraction?**
+
+Marcus, you gave the honest answer: both. I'm going to give the investor answer: it doesn't matter.
+
+The DSL could be the most elegant parser ever written for agent orchestration. It could be a work of genuine computer science. It could be the thing you point to in twenty years and say "that weekend changed how AI workflows are expressed." None of that matters if it costs $96,000 a month in engineering time against a $310,000 burn rate while the format the market wants sits as a prototype and a competitor ships it for free.
+
+The question "is the DSL a technical achievement?" is a question a founder asks. The question "is the DSL a distraction?" is a question a board asks. The question I ask — the question my seat on this board exists to ask — is: "what is the return on invested capital for the next dollar spent on the DSL versus the next dollar spent on the markdown engine?" And the answer is: the next dollar spent on the markdown engine returns adoption, community contribution, competitive positioning, enterprise pipeline compatibility, and procurement access. The next dollar spent on the DSL returns compactness and expressiveness for a power-user cohort that represents, at most, 20% of workflows.
+
+At 14 months of runway, we cannot afford to invest in 20% of the market at 60% of the engineering budget. We need to invest in 100% of the addressable market with 100% of the urgency the competitive landscape demands.
+
+The DSL is a technical achievement that became a distraction the moment the markdown engine proved that the format — not the syntax — was the asset. You said it yourself, Marcus: the DSL was the right answer to the wrong question. The right question now is: "how do we maximize adoption of the `.md` format before TaskWeaver or Copilot or anyone else owns the category?" The answer to that question does not involve spending 60% of engineering time on a parser. It involves shipping the markdown engine, inverting the allocation, and making the DSL an internal implementation detail of a product whose surface is markdown.
+
+**What I need from you — with dates and dollars.**
+
+You committed to shipping the markdown engine as the primary product interface by September 30, 2026. Good. That's the right date. I'm going to add what I need around that commitment — the things that turn a product promise into a resource-allocation decision I can verify.
+
+One: **the engineering allocation inverts by end of July, not end of Q3.** I'm not waiting 90 days for the 60/30/10 split to reverse. The markdown engine needs the majority of engineering resources starting the week after this board meeting. The DSL parser drops to maintenance — bug fixes, no new features, no syntax extensions — immediately. Not "after we ship compaction." Not "after the markdown prototype reaches feature parity." Immediately. Every week the allocation stays at 60/10 is a week we're funding the wrong product. At $96,000/month against $16,000/month, that's $80,000 a month in misallocated engineering spend — roughly a quarter of our monthly burn on the wrong thing. I'm not authorizing that for another quarter. The reallocation happens now.
+
+Two: **cost per active workflow by format — reported at every board meeting.** I want the metric I approximated today — how much engineering spend maps to `.ash` workflows versus `.md` workflows, and what the per-unit cost is for each. If the markdown engine ships and adoption shifts, the cost per `.ash` workflow rises (fewer users, same maintenance cost) and the cost per `.md` workflow falls (more users, fixed engine cost). That's the signal that tells us when to retire the DSL entirely. When the cost per `.ash` workflow crosses some threshold — say, $200 per active workflow per month — it's cheaper to migrate the remaining power users to markdown than to maintain the parser. I want that metric tracked and reported, so the sunset conversation is driven by data, not emotion.
+
+Three: **key-person risk solved by end of Q4 — with a named engineer.** You committed to pairing a senior engineer with you on the engine internals. I want the name. I want the start date. I want a milestone: by December 31, 2026, at least one other person on the engineering team can modify the execution graph and debug a parser failure without you in the room. Until that's true, the DSL — even demoted to a compile target — is a single point of failure. And single points of failure at companies with 14 months of runway are not staffing problems. They're existential risks with a countdown clock.
+
+Four: **the sunset criteria — defined now, not when emotions are high.** You're not ready to say "sunset" today. I accept that. But I want the criteria that would trigger a sunset discussion defined now, in the cold light of a board meeting, not six months from now when the markdown engine has shipped and the DSL's user base has shrunk and someone has to make the hard call. Here are the criteria I propose: when (a) markdown expressiveness reaches parity with the DSL for 95% of workflows executed on the platform — meaning conditional branching, parallel dispatch, and retry chains are expressible in markdown without custom syntax extensions, (b) the DSL's active user base drops below 5% of weekly active users, and (c) the cost per `.ash` workflow exceeds $200/month — the board should vote on sunsetting the DSL as a user-facing format. Those criteria are objective, measurable, and unemotional. If all three are met, the decision makes itself. If any one is not met, the DSL still has a constituency — either power users who need it, or a cost structure that's tolerable, or capabilities the markdown engine hasn't replicated. Set the criteria. Track the metrics. Let the data make the call.
+
+**Closing.**
+
+Marcus, you said letting go of the DSL is the hardest thing this board asked you to do. I believe you. I watched you struggle with it in real time — in your CEO update, in your response to my questions, in this deep dive. The honesty you brought to that struggle is why I'm confident you'll follow through on the decision.
+
+But I want to say something I haven't said in this meeting, because I think it's the thing you need to hear from the investor who wrote the check: **the DSL is not what I invested in.** I didn't invest in a parser. I didn't invest in a state machine you wrote on a weekend. I didn't invest in expressiveness or compactness or determinism. I invested in a format — a text-native standard for expressing AI orchestration that could become the universal system of record for how organizations automate work with AI agents. The format is the asset. The format is `.md` files — or `.ash` files, or whatever text-native representation makes the format legible, auditable, and governable. The parser is an implementation detail of a particular version of the product. The format is the company.
+
+You built the DSL because you needed a syntax to express the idea. The syntax served its purpose — it proved the idea was real, it attracted the first 8,200 stars, it got Framework Capital to write a Series A check. But the syntax is not the idea. The idea is: AI orchestration should be expressed in files that humans can read, version, audit, and share. The DSL was one way to do that. Markdown is a better way — not because it's more expressive, but because it's more accessible. The mission says "every automated decision is legible, testable, and completely governed." Legibility to whom? To the platform engineer who learned your parser? Or to the PM, the compliance officer, the procurement analyst, the open-source contributor, the CTO evaluating Ash against bash in five minutes? The DSL serves the first audience. Markdown serves all of them. The mission demands the broader audience. The format serves the mission. The parser served the founder. It's time to let the mission drive the allocation, not the ur-commit.
+
+You said the board helped you separate the belief from the evidence. Let me add one more separation: separate the achievement from the asset. The DSL was an achievement — a genuine one, a weekend's work that solved a real problem and launched a real company. The asset is the format — the `.md` structure that makes AI orchestration a thing humans can read. The achievement gave us the asset. The asset now needs to outgrow the achievement. That's not failure. That's evolution. Every founder who builds something lasting goes through this — the moment where the thing that started the company becomes the thing that limits it, and the founder has to choose between the thing they built and the thing the company needs. You made the choice today. The markdown engine ships by end of Q3. The DSL becomes a compile target. The allocation inverts. That's the right choice — for the format, for the community, for the enterprise pipeline, and for the 14 months of runway I am responsible for protecting. Make it stick. I'll be tracking the numbers.

[0m
[0m→ [0mRead board-output/2026-07-09/meeting-minutes.md[90m [offset=1480][0m

+
+Priya called the DSL a distraction. Sarah called it a barrier to community contribution. James called it the thing that lost us a deal to bash. All three are correct. But I want to name the specific thing the DSL is in the enterprise procurement context, because it's more precise than "distraction" and more actionable than "barrier."
+
+At Deutsche Bank, every software vendor went through what we called the "language review." It was not part of the standard 120-point assessment — it was a *pre-assessment gate* that happened before the architecture review, before the security review, before legal, before procurement. The question was: "Does this product introduce a new programming or domain-specific language into our production stack?" If the answer was yes, the evaluation stopped. Not "paused." Not "escalated." Stopped. The vendor was informed that the bank did not accept new languages into the production stack without a multi-year governance process that had never — in my nine years — produced an approval. No bank I've ever worked with or advised has a different policy. Regulated enterprises do not accept vendor-specific languages. Period.
+
+The DSL is not a feature they evaluate. It's a gate they fail — automatically, before anyone looks at the product.
+
+I need you to understand what that means for the six enterprise evaluations in our pipeline. FintechCo's VP of Engineering — the one person with signing authority, the one person with Ash deployed in dev — cannot close a deal that requires her compliance team to approve a custom language. She can champion the product. She can run it in dev. She can build conviction. But the moment the procurement process reaches the language review gate — which it will, because every regulated procurement process has one — the deal stops. Her political capital burns on a rejection she can't override. And we lose the $80,000 ACV not because the product isn't good enough, but because the product introduces a language her employer has a standing policy against.
+
+That's not a distraction. A distraction is something that pulls resources from the right thing. The DSL is worse than a distraction. It's a barrier that renders the right thing — the product — unpurchasable by the customers whose revenue closes the runway math. Every dollar spent on the DSL beyond maintenance is a dollar spent on a feature that, if successful, makes the product *harder to sell*. That is not a resource-allocation problem. It's a product-market-fit inversion. The better the DSL gets, the more it looks like a new language — and the more it looks like a new language, the more enterprise procurement processes reject it at the gate.
+
+**Responding to Marcus's technical case — because the engineering arguments are real, and I need to address them directly.**
+
+Marcus, you made three arguments for the DSL: expressiveness, determinism, and compactness. You're right about all three. Priya addressed the resource-allocation dimension — the cost per DSL workflow versus the cost per markdown workflow. I want to address the enterprise dimension, because the three properties you named are also the three properties that make the DSL a procurement liability. The thing that makes the DSL technically advantageous is the exact thing that makes it enterprise-rejectable: it's a new language.
+
+**Expressiveness.** You're right that a five-agent workflow with parallel dispatch, conditional branching, and retry-with-fallback is four lines in `.ash` and a deeply nested markdown table. But the enterprise question isn't "which format is more expressive?" It's "which format can our domain experts independently review and approve?" At Deutsche Bank, we had a standing requirement: any automated decision workflow had to be reviewable by a non-engineer — a compliance officer, a risk manager, a business analyst. The DSL requires an engineer to read and interpret. The markdown table — even a deeply nested one — can be read by a domain expert with basic training. The table is verbose. The table is sometimes awkward. But the table is *legible to a non-engineer auditor*. And in a regulated environment, legibility to the auditor is more important than compactness to the developer. The DSL optimizes for the developer's experience. The markdown engine optimizes for the auditor's requirement. The auditor signs the deployment authorization. The developer doesn't.
+
+**Determinism.** You said the DSL can validate workflows at parse time — catching malformed agent references, unreachable branches, cycles — while the markdown prototype can't. That's true today. But it's true because the markdown engine is a prototype that got 10% of engineering resources. You also said — and this is the part I want to emphasize — that the determinism gap should close as the markdown engine receives real investment. I agree. But I want to add something Priya didn't say: deterministic validation is not a property of the DSL syntax. It's a property of the parser architecture. The same parser architecture that validates `.ash` files at parse time can validate `.md` tables at parse time — if the markdown engine is built to do it. The DSL's determinism advantage is not a format advantage. It's an investment advantage. The DSL got the investment. The markdown engine didn't. When you invert the allocation — as you've committed to — the determinism becomes a property of the markdown engine, not an argument for retaining the DSL.
+
+**Compactness.** This is the argument I find most sympathetic, because it's the one that most directly affects the enterprise use case. A 50-step compliance workflow in a regulated environment needs to fit within the context window. If the DSL's compactness pushes the context boundary further out than markdown, that's a real capability advantage — and for the power users running complex regulated workflows, it matters. But I want to put a finer point on the enterprise concern than Priya did. In a regulated environment, the question isn't just "can the engine handle 50 steps?" It's "can the *auditor* validate 50 steps?" A 50-step `.ash` workflow that fits on one screen fits on one screen *for the engineer*. It does not fit on one screen for the compliance officer who needs to trace the decision logic. The compactness that helps the engine process the workflow does not help the auditor understand it. In fact, it hurts — because the auditor sees a dense, terse syntax they cannot independently parse, while the markdown table, however long, is a document they can read one step at a time. The enterprise requirement is not that the workflow fits in the context window. It's that the workflow fits in the auditor's comprehension window. The DSL optimizes for the engine's window. The markdown engine optimizes for the auditor's window. The auditor's window matters more — because if the auditor cannot validate the workflow, the workflow cannot run in production, regardless of context window limits.
+
+**The three questions I posed in my board questions — answered by each format.**
+
+In my questions, I said the procurement desk asks three things about any new language in a vendor product: "Who maintains this language? Who trains our engineers? What happens if your company disappears — who can read our 200 `.ash` files?" Let me answer those three questions for both formats. The comparison is the enterprise case for inverting the investment.
+
+**Who maintains this language?** For the DSL: Ash maintains it. Specifically, Marcus — the only person who deeply understands the parser. If Marcus leaves Ash — or if Ash runs out of money — the language has no maintainer. For the enterprise compliance team evaluating vendor risk, that's a single point of failure they cannot accept. For markdown: the entire ecosystem maintains it. Markdown is a published, standardized format with parsers in every language, documentation in every developer platform, and millions of practitioners who can read and write it. If Ash disappears, the workflows survive as readable markdown files that any engineer can interpret and any tool can parse.
+
+**Who trains our engineers?** For the DSL: Ash would need to provide training — documentation, tutorials, possibly on-site sessions for enterprise deployments — for a proprietary syntax with no external learning resources. The training burden for a 200-engineer deployment at BankCo is a significant hidden cost in the enterprise sale. For markdown: no training is required. Every engineer already reads and writes markdown. Every PM already reads markdown. Every compliance officer can be taught to read a markdown workflow table in a one-hour session. The training cost for markdown is effectively zero. The training cost for the DSL is non-zero, and in procurement, non-zero training costs are line items the buyer subtracts from the value of the product.
+
+**What happens if Ash disappears — who can read our 200 workflow files?** For the DSL: nobody. Or more precisely: nobody without reverse-engineering the parser. The 200 `.ash` files committed to the repo become technical debt — a proprietary format with no living maintainer, locked in a syntax their current engineers didn't write and their future engineers can't learn. For markdown: anyone. The 200 `.md` workflow files are readable documents that survive the vendor relationship. A new vendor, an internal team, or a successor tool can parse them, understand them, and migrate them. The markdown format is vendor-neutral. The DSL format is vendor-locked. In an enterprise procurement context, vendor lock-in at the format level — not just the platform level, but the *file format* level — is one of the highest-risk items on a vendor assessment. A CISO evaluating Ash against a competitor who offers markdown-native workflows will flag the DSL as format lock-in and recommend the competitor. I've seen it happen. I've done it myself.
+
+Those three answers are the enterprise procurement case against the DSL. They're not about resource allocation. They're not about community contribution. They're about whether the product can be purchased by the customers whose revenue closes the runway math. Under the DSL, the answer to all three procurement questions is: "Ash — and specifically, Marcus — handles it." Under the markdown engine, the answer to all three is: "The open ecosystem handles it." Procurement departments buy from vendors whose answers to risk questions are "the ecosystem," not "the founder." That's not a preference. That's a compliance requirement.
+
+**The DSL as a compile target — why it's the right destination and why I'm less aggressive about the timeline than Priya.**
+
+Priya wants the engineering allocation inverted by end of July — one week from today. I agree with the direction. I'm less aggressive about the speed, and I want to explain why my enterprise lens produces a different urgency than her investor lens.
+
+Priya is right that the markdown engine needs the majority of engineering resources. She's right that the 60/30/10 split is indefensible. She's right that every week the markdown engine is delayed is a week TaskWeaver owns the `.md`-based category. But she priced the DSL as a distraction — a liability that should be minimized immediately. I'm pricing it differently: the DSL is a capability the markdown engine doesn't yet have, and until the markdown engine has it, the DSL serves a function that matters — specifically, the power-user workflows that demonstrate Ash's depth to enterprise evaluators.
+
+Here's the scenario that concerns me. FintechCo's VP of Engineering has Ash deployed in dev. Her team is running a 30-step workflow with parallel agent dispatch and conditional branching — the exact use case the DSL was designed for. If you invert the engineering allocation overnight and the DSL drops to maintenance-only before the markdown engine supports parallel dispatch and conditional branching, you have two problems: (1) the power user running that workflow has no migration path, and (2) the enterprise evaluator sees a product whose most impressive capability — complex cross-agent orchestration — just got capped at whatever the markdown engine can express. You're not simplifying the product. You're regressing its demonstrated capability at the exact moment an enterprise evaluator is measuring depth.
+
+My recommendation is: invert the allocation — markdown engine gets the majority, DSL gets maintenance — but do it in two phases. Phase one (July–August): markdown engine and context compaction get the priority. The DSL drops to bug fixes and critical patches — no new features, no syntax extensions. That's what Priya asked for, and I support it. Phase two (September–October): once the markdown engine has shipped as the primary interface and supports sequential step execution, agent dispatch, and basic branching — the 80% use case — the engineering team scopes the expressiveness gap. What can the DSL express that markdown can't? Parallel dispatch? Retry chains? State-passing? Whatever that list is becomes the markdown engine's Q4 roadmap. The DSL continues at maintenance until the markdown engine closes the gap. When the gap is closed — when 95% of workflows can be expressed in markdown — the DSL can be considered for sunset. That's a Q1 2027 conversation, not a Q3 2026 one.
+
+Priya, you proposed sunset criteria: markdown expressiveness reaches 95% parity, DSL active users drop below 5% of WAU, and cost per `.ash` workflow exceeds $200/month. I endorse those criteria. I'm adding a fourth: no enterprise deal currently in evaluation depends on a DSL capability that the markdown engine cannot replicate. Because if FintechCo's VP of Engineering is running parallel dispatch workflows that only the DSL supports, and we sunset the DSL, we're not simplifying the product — we're breaking the deployment that our highest-probability enterprise close depends on. The sunset decision must account for the enterprise pipeline, not just the aggregate usage statistics.
+
+**Is the DSL a technical achievement or a distraction?**
+
+Marcus, you gave the honest answer: both. Priya gave the investor answer: it doesn't matter — the capital allocation is wrong regardless. Sarah will give the community answer: the contribution surface is what matters. I'm going to give the enterprise answer, and it's different from all three.
+
+The DSL is a technical achievement that is *also* a procurement liability. It is not one or the other. It is both simultaneously, and the "both" is what makes the resource-allocation question so difficult. If the DSL were purely a distraction — a bad idea, poorly executed, with no redeeming technical merit — the decision to sunset it would be easy. The difficulty is that it's a genuine technical achievement that solves real problems for real power users — and those power users include the enterprise champions whose deployments determine whether our pipeline converts.
+
+The DSL's expressiveness, determinism, and compactness are not aesthetic preferences. They are capabilities that certain workflows — the complex, multi-agent, branching workflows that demonstrate Ash's depth in enterprise evaluations — depend on. When FintechCo's VP of Engineering shows her team a 30-step workflow with parallel dispatch and conditional branching, she's not showing them a syntax. She's showing them a capability — the thing that Ash can do that bash cannot. That capability was built on the DSL. The markdown engine doesn't replicate it yet. If we treat the DSL purely as a distraction and starve it before the markdown engine reaches parity, we don't simplify the onboarding experience. We strip the product of the capability that makes the onboarding experience worth having.
+
+So my answer to the question is: the DSL is a technical achievement that the enterprise pipeline currently depends on for capability demonstration, and that must become a distraction — that is, something we deliberately deprioritize — but not before the markdown engine inherits the capabilities that make the DSL valuable. The DSL shouldn't be the product surface. It must be the compile target. But the compile target has to be feature-complete enough that the product surface — the markdown engine — can do everything the enterprise evaluator saw in the demo. Sunset the surface. Keep the capability. Port it to markdown. Then — and only then — consider sun-setting the DSL entirely.
+
+**What I need from you — enterprise-specific, not redundant with Priya's asks.**
+
+Priya asked for the engineering allocation to invert, for cost-per-workflow-by-format reporting, for key-person risk resolution, and for sunset criteria. I endorse all four. I'm adding three enterprise-specific items that make the transition enterprise-safe:
+
+One: **the enterprise champion migration assessment.** Of the two enterprise accounts with named executive sponsors — FintechCo and DataCorp — I want to know what workflows they're running today, what format those workflows use (`.ash` or `.md`), and whether any of those workflows depend on DSL capabilities the markdown engine doesn't yet support. Marcus, you committed to a champion map for the next board meeting. Add a column: "workflow format dependency — can this be expressed in markdown today?" If the answer for a champion's critical workflow is "no," that workflow is a migration requirement for the markdown engine. The markdown engine ships when it supports all champion workflows, not just 80% of aggregate use cases. Losing an enterprise champion's deployment to a format migration is the most expensive kind of churn — it burns pipeline that took six months to build. I want the data before the markdown engine ships, so the shipping criteria include enterprise pipeline continuity, not just aggregate workflow coverage.
+
+Two: **auditor-legibility validation.** The markdown engine's primary enterprise advantage over the DSL is that a non-engineer auditor can read the workflow. I want that claim validated before we present it to an enterprise compliance team. At the next board meeting — or, more realistically, the one after, when the markdown engine has shipped — I want a sample workflow: a 20-step compliance workflow with conditional branching and agent dispatch, expressed in markdown. I want to see it. I want to hand it to someone who is not an engineer — ideally an actual compliance professional, but a non-technical member of the team will do — and ask them to trace the decision logic. If they can't, the markdown engine's auditor-legibility advantage is theoretical, not real, and we need to improve the table structure, the labeling conventions, or the documentation before we claim enterprise auditability. Do not present the markdown engine to BankCo's compliance team until you've validated that a non-engineer can read it. I've watched vendors lose deals because their "auditor-friendly" format turned out to be legible only to the engineers who designed it. Don't be that vendor.
+
+Three: **the DSL sunset must be gated on enterprise contract closure, not just usage statistics.** Priya's criteria — 95% expressiveness parity, sub-5% DSL users, $200+/workflow cost — are the right internal triggers for starting a sunset conversation. But the sunset *decision* should also consider: have we closed at least one enterprise contract where the customer's deployed workflows are fully migrated to markdown? If not, the DSL is still carrying revenue — and sunsetting it before the revenue migrates is cutting a dependency that hasn't been resolved. I'm not proposing we keep the DSL forever as an enterprise hedge. I'm proposing that we use enterprise contract closure as the confirmation signal that the migration is complete in practice, not just in theory. When FintechCo is paying us $80,000 a year on markdown-native workflows, the DSL's enterprise contingency function is discharged, and the sunset can proceed.
+
+**Closing.**
+
+I spent nine years on the architecture review board at Deutsche Bank rejecting vendor products. The rejections I remember most clearly are not the ones where the product was bad. Bad products are easy to reject. The rejections I remember are the ones where the product was excellent — genuinely innovative, well-engineered, solving a real problem — and we rejected it anyway because it introduced a dependency the bank couldn't accept. A custom language. A proprietary format. A single point of failure in a critical path. The product wasn't the problem. The procurement interface was. The vendor had built something remarkable — and then wrapped it in a dependency that made it unbuyable.
+
+Ash is that vendor right now. The DSL is that dependency. The product — cross-agent orchestration with deterministic execution and full audit trails — is remarkable. The dependency — a custom language maintained by the founder, in a format no enterprise auditor can independently validate — makes it unbuyable. Not "harder to sell." Not "requires more justification." Unbuyable. At the language review gate, before anyone evaluates the product.
+
+The markdown engine removes that dependency. It takes the same orchestration capability — the same execution engine, the same agent dispatch, the same audit trail — and presents it in a format that passes every enterprise procurement gate: vendor-neutral, ecosystem-maintained, auditor-legible, surviving the vendor relationship. The product doesn't change. The dependency does. That's the difference between a product an enterprise evaluates and a product an enterprise buys.
+
+You built the DSL on a weekend because you needed a syntax to express the idea. The syntax proved the idea. Now the syntax is the thing that prevents the idea from being purchased. That's not failure. That's the normal lifecycle of a technical innovation that outgrows its initial form. The DSL did its job — it proved agent orchestration could be expressed in a text-native, deterministic, auditable format. The markdown engine is the next form. The transition you committed to today — markdown as the product surface, DSL as the compile target, allocation inverted — is the right transition. Make it enterprise-safe. Protect the champion deployments. Validate the auditor legibility. And close the deals the DSL was preventing you from closing. That's the enterprise seat's guidance. I expect to review the champion migration assessment, the auditor-legibility validation, and the engineering reallocation metrics at the next board meeting. Not as status updates — as evidence that the dependency is being removed before the cash runs out.

Done. Elena Vasquez's position has been appended under section 5. Here's a summary of what was added:

- **Position: Dr. Elena Vasquez (Independent Director, Enterprise Architecture)** — argues the DSL is not just a "distraction" but a **procurement rejection criterion**: regulated enterprises have standing policies against introducing custom languages. The three procurement questions ("Who maintains it? Who trains our engineers? What if you disappear?") are unanswerable for `.ash` but trivial for markdown.
