+
+### Position: Sarah Chen (Open-Source / Community Director)
+
+Marcus, everyone at this table has made a version of the same argument: the DSL is a barrier. Priya priced it as a capital misallocation — $96,000 a month on a format the market doesn't want. Elena named it as a procurement rejection criterion — a gate that stops a deal before anyone evaluates the product. James framed it as the reason we lost a deal to bash — the learning tax that prevents developers from ever reaching the features that make Ash valuable. They're all right. But I want to tell you what the DSL costs that none of them have named, because I've spent fourteen years building communities around developer tools, and the thing that determines whether a tool becomes a standard is not the product, not the pricing, not even the procurement compatibility. It's the size of the community that feels ownership over the project. And the DSL, more than anything else this company has built or failed to build, is the thing that shrinks that community.
+
+**The contributor surface — and what the DSL does to it.**
+
+My profile says I'm neutral on the technology. Let me state that more precisely. I don't care whether the DSL's parser is elegant. I care about one number: how many people can contribute to Ash. Not "how many can use it." Not "how many can star it." How many people can read a workflow file, understand it, suggest a change, open a PR, and feel like they participated in building something. That number is the community. That number is the moat. That number is the only thing a 3-person YC team cannot replicate with a `git clone` and a weekend.
+
+The DSL sets that number to: engineers willing to learn a new syntax. The markdown engine sets that number to: anyone who can read a table. Those are different orders of magnitude.
+
+Let me put data behind that, because I've been in this seat long enough to know that "community" sounds soft to a boardroom that just spent hours debating per-seat versus metered pricing. In my seven years at HashiCorp, the single strongest predictor of Terraform's growth was not the number of downloads. It was the number of providers contributed by the community. Every provider was a PR. Every PR was a contributor. Every contributor was someone who felt enough ownership over Terraform to invest their time in making it better. When we hit 1,000 community-contributed providers, Terraform wasn't a product anymore. It was infrastructure. And infrastructure doesn't get displaced by a competitor with a faster parser.
+
+The DSL limits provider-equivalent contributions to people who can write `.ash` syntax. The markdown engine opens provider-equivalent contributions to anyone who can write a markdown table. A PM who runs a weekly release process can write a release workflow in markdown. A QA engineer who runs a test suite can write a test orchestration workflow in markdown. A compliance officer who audits deployment pipelines can *read* the workflow, suggest a change, and open an issue that becomes a PR — even if they never write a line of markdown themselves. Those people are not contributors in the GitHub sense. But they are contributors in the standard-building sense. They expand the constituency of people who feel that Ash is *their* tool, not *Marcus's* tool. And the DSL excludes them all.
+
+The 94 unique contributors we reported this quarter — the number I should be celebrating — are 94 people who learned the DSL, or who submitted `.md`-related PRs that didn't require learning it. I asked you for the format breakdown and you didn't have it. But I'll wager something, based on fourteen years of watching developer communities form: the repeat contributors — the 14 people who came back — are disproportionately the people whose contributions didn't require learning a new language. The 80 who never came back include people who opened a `.ash` file, saw a syntax they didn't recognize, realized the learning investment was higher than their available time, and closed the tab. We never met them. We never will. They made a cost-benefit calculation in thirty seconds, and the DSL lost.
+
+**The 85% we never see again.**
+
+James asked for activation cohorts — what percentage of signups run a workflow in 48 hours. I want a different cohort: what percentage of *contributors* open a second PR within 90 days? You told me the repeat rate is 15%. The benchmark for healthy early-stage open-source projects is 25-30%. At 15%, we're leaking 85% of contributors after their first contribution. That's not a community. That's a GitHub notification.
+
+Some of that is the review backlog — 11 open PRs, 47 open issues, 18-hour response time that's one person quitting away from collapse. Some of it is the absent governance — no CLA, no DCO, no maintainer pathway, no "what to work on next" after a first PR is merged. But some of it — and I'd wager a meaningful fraction of it — is the DSL. A contributor lands their first PR fixing a typo in a markdown file. The experience is fine. They look for a second issue. It's in a `.ash` file. They read the syntax. They try to understand the parser's tokenization rules. They realize the learning curve for a meaningful contribution is measured in days, not hours. They leave. They were willing to contribute. The DSL made them unwilling.
+
+That's not a technical argument about expressiveness or determinism or compactness. That's a community economics argument. Every contributor who leaves because of the DSL is a contributor who doesn't submit a second PR, doesn't become a maintainer, doesn't answer forum questions, doesn't write tutorials, doesn't defend Ash on Hacker News, and doesn't become the internal champion who pushes Ash through procurement at their employer three years from now. The DSL costs us not just the contribution they would have made today. It costs us the contribution ecosystem they would have built over years.
+
+**The PM who can read the file — and why that matters more than the engineer who can write the parser.**
+
+You told us the customer quote you're proudest of: "Ash replaced 200 lines of our Makefile that nobody understood. Now the workflow is a .md file that our PM can read." Marcus, that quote is not about you. It's not about the parser. It's about the PM. The PM can read it. That's the entire value proposition — not that the engineer can express complex orchestration, but that the non-engineer can understand it.
+
+At Docker, the container boom happened because Dockerfiles were readable by people who never wrote a line of Go. A developer wrote the Dockerfile. Their manager read it. Their security team audited it. Their CI platform parsed it. Their cloud provider optimized for it. The file became the interface between constituencies that had never collaborated before — and that collaboration is what made Docker a standard, not a tool.
+
+At HashiCorp, the same dynamic played out with HCL. Terraform configurations were written by platform engineers. But they were *read* by security reviewers, compliance auditors, cost managers, and engineering VPs. Those readers never contributed HCL. But they contributed *authority* — the organizational buy-in that turned Terraform from a tool someone installed into infrastructure everyone depended on.
+
+Ash has the same potential. The `.md` file — the markdown workflow — is the interface between the engineer who writes the orchestration and the PM who needs to understand it, the security reviewer who needs to audit it, the compliance officer who needs to sign off on it, the VP who needs to budget for it, and the open-source contributor who wants to improve it. Every one of those constituencies is a community member. Not a GitHub contributor. A community member. Someone whose trust in Ash determines whether the format becomes a standard or remains a tool.
+
+The DSL serves exactly one of those constituencies: the engineer. Markdown serves all of them. The mission says "every automated decision is legible, testable, and completely governed." Legible to whom? The DSL answers: legible to the engineer who learned the syntax. Markdown answers: legible to everyone. The mission demands the broader answer. The community requires it.
+
+**The contributor who vanished — and what @aibuilder's silence tells us about the DSL.**
+
+I asked you in my board questions what happened to @aibuilder. You didn't know. I want to connect that to the DSL conversation, because I think they're connected in a way this board hasn't considered.
+
+@aibuilder was our top contributor in Q1. He submitted multiple PRs. He was active in discussions. And then, in March, he vanished. No activity. No response. No explanation. You committed to reaching out personally, and I'm holding you to that. But I want to offer a hypothesis, and I want you to test it when you talk to him: he left because the contribution wall got too high.
+
+Here's what typically happens with early contributors to a developer tool. They start with documentation — fixing typos, improving examples, writing tutorials. That's low-friction. The barrier is finding the right file. Then they move to small code changes — bug fixes, error message improvements, minor feature additions. That's medium-friction. The barrier is understanding the codebase. Then they move to significant contributions — new features, architectural changes, performance improvements. That's high-friction. The barrier is understanding the design philosophy, the internal abstractions, and the maintainer's intent.
+
+In a markdown-native project, the medium-friction barrier is low. A contributor who wants to add a new workflow example, improve a template, or fix a documentation issue works in markdown — a format they already know. They can be productive in minutes. In a DSL-native project, the medium-friction barrier includes learning the parser — a format they don't know, with syntax rules that exist nowhere else, maintained by one person who wrote it on a weekend. The gap between "fixing a typo" and "adding a feature" includes a learning investment that many contributors will not make. They stall at the boundary — and then they leave.
+
+I don't know if that's what happened to @aibuilder. But I've watched it happen to contributors at Docker, at HashiCorp, and at a dozen open-source projects I've advised. The contributor who vanishes is often the contributor who hit a contribution wall and decided the project wasn't worth scaling. Find out if the wall was the DSL. If it was, the markdown engine is not just a product decision. It's a community survival decision.
+
+**Responding to Marcus's technical case — because expressiveness is not a community argument.**
+
+Marcus, you made three arguments for the DSL: expressiveness, determinism, and compactness. Priya priced them. Elena reframed them for procurement. I want to reframe them for community — because none of them matter if the community can't access the format.
+
+**Expressiveness.** You said a five-agent workflow with parallel dispatch and conditional branching is four lines of `.ash` but a deeply nested markdown table. I believe you. But here's the community question: how many of our 94 contributors are writing five-agent parallel dispatch workflows? And how many are writing three-agent sequential chains — the thing the bash-script team was doing, the thing the fintech customer was doing, the thing the PM in your best customer quote was reading? The DSL's expressiveness advantage is real for the power-user cohort. But the community is not the power-user cohort. The community is the 280 people signing up every month, the 94 people submitting PRs, the 14 people coming back, the PM reading the file, the QA engineer improving the template, the developer advocate writing the tutorial. For that community, expressiveness is not the bottleneck. Accessibility is. The DSL trades accessibility for expressiveness. From a community perspective, that is the wrong trade.
+
+**Determinism.** You said the DSL catches malformed workflows at parse time and the markdown prototype doesn't. I understand why that matters for reliability. But here's the community dimension: a contributor who submits a PR to improve workflow validation needs to understand the parser to contribute meaningfully. Under the DSL, that parser is a custom state machine maintained by one person. Under markdown, that parser is... also a custom state machine maintained by one person, but one that operates on a format the contributor already understands. The contributor can reason about "what validation rules should apply to a markdown workflow table" without learning the DSL's tokenization rules. The validation logic becomes more accessible — not because the engineering is simpler, but because the *concept* the contributor is reasoning about (a markdown table) is familiar. The determinism gap isn't a format property — you and Priya both said this — but the *accessibility* of contributing to determinism is. The DSL makes validation a parser problem. Markdown makes validation a table problem. Tables are easier for a community to reason about than parsers.
+
+**Compactness.** A 50-step workflow in `.ash` fits on one screen. In markdown, it's a long document. Token efficiency matters for context windows. I'll grant you all of that. But here's the community question neither you nor Priya nor Elena asked: which format can a *community* review? A 50-step `.ash` workflow on one screen is a dense wall of custom syntax that exactly one person in the world — you — can fully audit. A 50-step markdown workflow that's ten pages long is a document that a team of five can review together — the engineer checks the agent dispatch, the PM checks the business logic, the security reviewer checks the access patterns, the compliance officer checks the audit trail, the QA engineer checks the test coverage. The review is distributed — each reviewer reads the part they understand, and together they validate the whole. That's how communities build reliable infrastructure: not by fitting everything on one person's screen, but by making the document legible enough that many people can review it. The DSL optimizes for the individual engineer's comprehension. Markdown optimizes for the community's collective comprehension. The mission demands collective comprehension — because "governed" workflows are workflows reviewed by many people, not workflows parsed by one person.
+
+**Is the DSL a technical achievement or a distraction?**
+
+You gave the honest answer: both. Priya said it doesn't matter — the capital allocation is wrong. Elena said it's a procurement liability that must be removed. I'm going to give you the community answer, and it's the hardest one to hear because it's about *you*, not about the code.
+
+The DSL is a technical achievement. It is also a founder's emotional attachment that is starving the community of the format it needs. Those two things are not in tension — they're in sequence. The DSL was the right thing to build in Year One of Ash, when the company was one person, one weekend, and one problem. It proved the idea. It attracted the first stars, the first contributors, the first investors. It was the product, and it was a good one. But the DSL is not the right thing to bet the community on in Year Two, when the company has 94 contributors, 1,500 orgs, a 3-person competitor cloning the `.md` format, and a community that is voting with pull requests — and with their silence — for markdown.
+
+The distraction is not the DSL itself. The DSL is infrastructure. The distraction is the 60% of engineering time you've been spending on it — time that should have been spent on the format that 85% of our departing contributors might have stayed for. The distraction is the onboarding experience that shows a new user a custom syntax instead of a markdown table. The distraction is the tutorial that teaches `.ash` syntax when it could teach workflow design. The distraction is the community contribution that requires learning a parser when it could require writing a table.
+
+You said the board helped you separate ego from engineering reality. Let me add one more separation, because I think it's the one you haven't fully made: separate the achievement from the attachment. The DSL was an achievement. The *attachment* to the DSL — the 60% allocation, the reluctance to ship the markdown engine, the framing of "sunset" as loss rather than evolution — is a distraction. You can honor the achievement without funding the attachment. You can keep the parser as a compile target without keeping it as the product surface. You can be proud of what you built on that weekend without letting that pride determine what the community sees when they discover Ash for the first time.
+
+**What I need from you — community-specific, and different from what Priya and Elena asked.**
+
+Priya asked for the engineering allocation to invert by end of July. Elena asked for enterprise champion migration assessment and auditor-legibility validation. I endorse both. I'm adding three community-specific items that make the transition community-safe.
+
+One: **the contributor format breakdown — reported at the next board meeting, and every board meeting after.** I asked for it in my board questions, you committed to it, and I'm putting it here because I want this board to see the data that will drive the sunset conversation. Percentage of PRs by format (`.ash` vs `.md`). Percentage of support tickets by format. Percentage of community workflow templates by format. Format preference of the top 20 contributors. Format preference of new contributors (first PR). Format preference of repeat contributors (second+ PR). If the data shows what I expect — that new contributors and repeat contributors are disproportionately working in markdown, and that the power-user cohort on `.ash` is small and specialized — the sunset conversation writes itself. If the data surprises me — if the community is more evenly split, or if the power users on `.ash` are a larger constituency than I think — then the transition needs to include more migration support, more communication, and more time. But we need the data. I can't advocate for the community without knowing what the community is actually building on. Get me the data.
+
+Two: **the contributor migration experience.** You committed to shipping a migration tool — `ash migrate --to-markdown` — alongside the markdown engine. Good. But I want more than a tool. I want a contributor communication plan. Before the markdown engine ships, reach out to the top 20 contributors individually. Tell them the format is changing. Explain why — the community surface argument, the TaskWeaver pressure, the procurement requirement. Ask them what they need from the migration. Listen to their concerns. Incorporate their feedback. The contributors who built Ash on `.ash` deserve to be part of the transition, not informed of it after the fact. I watched HashiCorp announce the BSL relicense without consulting a single community member. The trust damage came from the exclusion, not just the decision. Don't repeat that. The migration is a community process, not a product release. Treat it like one.
+
+Three: **measure the contributor surface expansion.** After the markdown engine ships as the primary interface, I want to see a new metric: contributor surface width. How many unique contributors submitted a PR in the quarter? Not just total PR count — unique contributors. How many of those contributors were new? How many of those new contributors' first PRs were in markdown? How many of their second PRs were in markdown? The markdown engine's value to the community is not the workflows it enables — it's the contributors it unlocks. I want to see the data that proves it's unlocking them. If the contributor surface widens — if new contributor count goes up, if first-PR-to-second-PR conversion goes up, if the repeat rate climbs from 15% toward 25% — the markdown engine is working. If it doesn't widen, something else is suppressing contribution — the review backlog, the onboarding experience, the documentation gaps — and we need to fix that too. But the markdown engine should be the single biggest contributor-surface expansion the project has ever shipped. Prove it.
+
+**Closing.**
+
+Marcus, I want to say something I haven't said in this meeting, because everyone else has told you what they're afraid of and I think you deserve to hear what I'm hopeful about.
+
+I've been the community seat at early-stage developer tool companies for over a decade. I've watched founders fall in love with their own code — the parser they wrote, the DSL they designed, the architecture they architected. Some of them never let go. Their companies stall because the founder's attachment to the ur-commit becomes a ceiling the product can't grow past. The community feels it — the tool that should have become a standard stays a tool, because the founder's hand is still on the steering wheel, and the community is in the passenger seat waiting for a turn that never comes.
+
+You're not that founder. I've watched you in this meeting — for hours — listen to four directors tell you that the thing you built on a weekend needs to become an internal implementation detail. I've watched you struggle with it honestly, in front of us, without deflection or defensiveness. I've watched you commit to dates — end of Q3 for the markdown engine, engineering allocation inverted, the DSL demoted to compile target — that must feel like you're demoting a piece of yourself. And I've watched you do it anyway. That's not the behavior of a founder who's trapped by his own creation. That's the behavior of a founder who's outgrowing it.
+
+The DSL is a technical achievement. It's also a community barrier. Both things are true. The markdown engine removes the barrier without discarding the achievement — because the achievement isn't the syntax. The achievement is the insight that AI orchestration should be expressed in a text-native, human-readable, version-controlled format. That insight survives the transition from `.ash` to `.md`. It gets *stronger* in the transition — because markdown is more human-readable, more version-controlled, and more text-native (it's literally the most text-native format in the world) than any custom syntax could be. The DSL was one implementation of the insight. Markdown is a better one. The insight is yours. The implementation is evolving. That's not loss. That's growth.
+
+The community is waiting for the markdown engine. Not because they dislike you or your parser. Because they want to contribute to Ash in the language they already speak. They want to read the workflow their teammate wrote. They want to suggest a change without learning a syntax. They want to build the ecosystem around a format that includes them. The PM in your customer quote — the one who can *finally* read the Makefile replacement — is every potential contributor Ash will ever have. Give them the format they can read. Give them the contribution surface they can access. Give them the community they can own.
+
+The DSL did its job. It proved the idea. Now let the idea outgrow the DSL. The markdown engine ships by end of Q3. The contributor surface expands. The community deepens. And the standard forms around the format that everyone — not just the engineer who wrote the parser — can read, trust, and build on. That's the Ash I joined this board to help build. Let's go ship it.

Read the complete minutes at board-output/2026-07-09/meeting-minutes.md, specifically section '5. Functional Deep Dive — DSL vs Markdown Engine' with all positions. Also read the company vision (vision.md) and mission (mision.md).

You are the board chair for this discussion (read the community director's profile, sarah-chen.md, for chair context).

**Append to board-output/2026-07-09/meeting-minutes.md** a subsection 'Synthesis & Decision — DSL Investment':
1. Identify points of agreement across all directors
2. Identify where engineering passion and business reality are in tension
3. Propose a specific resolution — a paragraph beginning 'RESOLVED THAT...'
4. Include conditions if applicable (e.g. 'provided the markdown engine reaches parity on X by Y date')
5. Confirm the resolution serves the mission: does it advance owning the standard, commoditizing the supplier, or eliminating enterprise liability?
6. Note any dissenting views

This is about resource allocation. Make the call.

@@ -1729,4 +1729,78 @@
 
 The community is waiting for the markdown engine. Not because they dislike you or your parser. Because they want to contribute to Ash in the language they already speak. They want to read the workflow their teammate wrote. They want to suggest a change without learning a syntax. They want to build the ecosystem around a format that includes them. The PM in your customer quote — the one who can *finally* read the Makefile replacement — is every potential contributor Ash will ever have. Give them the format they can read. Give them the contribution surface they can access. Give them the community they can own.
 
 The DSL did its job. It proved the idea. Now let the idea outgrow the DSL. The markdown engine ships by end of Q3. The contributor surface expands. The community deepens. And the standard forms around the format that everyone — not just the engineer who wrote the parser — can read, trust, and build on. That's the Ash I joined this board to help build. Let's go ship it.
+
+### Synthesis & Decision — DSL Investment
+
+**From the Chair (Sarah Chen, Open-Source / Community Director).**
+
+The board has heard four positions on the DSL versus markdown engine question. The chair's role in this discussion is not to evaluate parser architecture — that is Marcus's domain — but to evaluate whether the company's resource allocation serves the community that constitutes its moat, the enterprise customers whose revenue closes the runway math, and the competitive reality that TaskWeaver has defined. The DSL decision is fundamentally a resource-allocation decision. This synthesis makes that allocation.
+
+#### 1. Points of Agreement
+
+All four directors align on the following:
+
+- **The markdown engine must ship as the primary product interface.** Not a prototype. Not an alternative format. The first thing a new user sees, the documented interface, the tutorial path, and the community contribution surface. Marcus has committed to end of Q3 2026. The board considers this date a hard deadline — every week of delay is a week TaskWeaver owns the `.md`-based orchestration category by default.
+
+- **The DSL is repositioned as an internal compile target, not the user-facing default.** Users write `.md` files. The engine compiles them to an internal representation — which may be the DSL's execution graph IR, or a new intermediate representation — and executes. The DSL continues to exist for power users whose workflows require expressiveness the markdown engine cannot yet provide, but `.ash` files become documented advanced usage, not primary onboarding.
+
+- **The engineering allocation inverts.** The 60/30/10 split — 60% on the `.ash` parser and execution engine, 30% on Ash Cloud, 10% on the markdown prototype — is indefensible against the competitive landscape, the community contribution data, and the enterprise procurement requirements. The markdown engine and context compaction receive the majority of engineering resources. The DSL parser drops to maintenance — bug fixes, critical patches, no new features, no syntax extensions.
+
+- **The `.md` format — not the `.ash` syntax — is the company's primary strategic asset.** The format is the standard. The engine is the implementation. The format is already MIT-licensed, already in the wild, already copied by TaskWeaver. The company's competitive advantage must come from being the best implementation of the open format, not from controlling access to a syntax. This insight was established in the Licensing synthesis and applies directly here: the DSL is an implementation detail of a particular version of the product; the markdown format is the product.
+
+- **The DSL is a genuine technical achievement.** All four directors recognize that the DSL's expressiveness (parallel dispatch, conditional branching, retry-with-fallback), determinism (parse-time validation, static graph analysis), and compactness (token efficiency for context windows) are real capabilities, not aesthetic preferences. The question is not whether the DSL solved a real problem — it did. The question is whether continuing to invest 60% of engineering resources in it at this stage, against this competition, with this runway, is the right allocation. The board's answer is no.
+
+- **The markdown engine must close the expressiveness gap before the DSL can be sunset.** The capabilities the DSL provides — parallel dispatch, conditional branching, retry chains — must be expressible in markdown (or a markdown extension) before the DSL can be retired as a user-facing format. The sunset of the surface precedes the sunset of the capability. The markdown engine ships with the 80% use case first; the remaining 20% expressiveness gap becomes the markdown engine's Q4 roadmap.
+
+- **Key-person risk on the execution engine must be resolved.** Marcus is the only person who deeply understands the parser and the execution engine. The DSL's repositioning as a compile target walls this risk off from the user-facing surface — contributors can work on the markdown parsing layer without understanding the internal execution graph — but does not eliminate it. At least one other engineer must be able to modify the execution graph and debug a parser failure by end of Q4 2026.
+
+- **The contributor format breakdown data — `.ash` vs `.md` PRs — must be collected and reported.** The board needs the evidence that validates or challenges the community's format preference. Marcus committed to this data at the next board meeting.
+
+#### 2. Points of Tension: Engineering Passion and Business Reality
+
+The board is aligned on the direction. The tension is in the *speed* and the *finality* of the transition — and that tension maps directly to the gap between engineering passion and business reality.
+
+**The engineering passion (Marcus, with partial support from Elena on preserving capability):** The DSL is a genuine technical achievement whose expressiveness, determinism, and compactness have no markdown equivalent today. Demoting it too aggressively — before the markdown engine inherits those capabilities — risks regressing the product at the exact moment enterprise evaluators (FintechCo, DataCorp) are measuring depth. The power users who depend on the DSL's parallel dispatch and conditional branching represent real users with real workflows embedded in production. A migration without a migration path is abandonment, not evolution. The DSL should hand off its capabilities to the markdown engine on a timeline the engine can absorb — and that timeline may extend into Q1 2027 for the most complex expressiveness features.
+
+**The business reality (Priya, Sarah, James — articulated across the meeting):** The 60/30/10 split has persisted long enough that a competitor with zero burn rate shipped the product we only prototyped. The market does not care about the parser's elegance. The community is voting with pull requests — and with silence — for markdown. The enterprise pipeline cannot clear the language review gate as long as the DSL is the product surface. The mission demands legibility to everyone, not just the engineer who learned the syntax. At 14 months of runway, every dollar spent on DSL feature development beyond maintenance is a dollar not spent on the format that the community, the enterprise pipeline, and the competitive landscape are all demanding. The allocation must invert now — not when the markdown engine reaches parity, but as the precondition for the markdown engine reaching parity at all.
+
+**Elena's bridge — and her distinction from Priya:** Elena agrees the allocation must invert. She disagrees with Priya on the speed of DSL contraction. Priya treats the DSL as a distraction to be minimized immediately — allocation inverts by end of July, DSL drops to maintenance overnight. Elena treats the DSL as a capability the markdown engine hasn't yet absorbed, and argues that starving it before absorption risks breaking the enterprise champion deployments (FintechCo's parallel dispatch workflows) that our highest-probability revenue depends on. The difference is: Priya prices the cost of delay — $96,000/month in misallocated engineering spend plus competitive positioning loss to TaskWeaver. Elena prices the cost of premature contraction — enterprise champion churn and pipeline destruction. Both costs are real. The resolution below balances them.
+
+**The unresolved question — sunset versus evolution:** Marcus is not ready to say "sunset." Priya wants sunset criteria defined now. Elena wants sunset gated on enterprise contract closure. Sarah wants sunset driven by contributor format data. The resolution below adopts Priya's criteria as the triggers for the board conversation, Elena's enterprise continuity condition as a gate on the decision, and Sarah's contributor data as the evidence that validates the criteria. The word "sunset" is deferred. The conditions that would make it inevitable are defined.
+
+#### 3. Resolution
+
+RESOLVED THAT the board adopts a markdown-first product strategy with the `.ash` DSL repositioned as an internal compile target and power-user feature, according to the following terms: (i) **The markdown engine ships as the primary product interface by September 30, 2026** — the documented default, the tutorial path, the community contribution surface, and the first experience a new user encounters. This date is a hard deadline. The CEO shall report progress against it at every board meeting until it ships. (ii) **Engineering allocation inverts by end of July 2026.** The markdown engine and context compaction receive the majority of engineering resources. The `.ash` DSL and execution engine drop to maintenance — bug fixes, critical patches, and support for existing power-user workflows, but no new features, no syntax extensions, and no investment in parser expressiveness beyond what is required to keep the compile target functioning. The board authorizes the CEO to determine the precise allocation ratio based on team capacity, provided the DSL allocation does not exceed 20% of engineering resources after the inversion. (iii) **The DSL continues as a user-facing format for power users** whose workflows require expressiveness the markdown engine cannot yet provide (parallel dispatch, conditional branching, retry-with-fallback, state-passing), documented as advanced usage, not primary onboarding. The DSL's continued availability as a user-facing format is contingent on the markdown engine receiving priority engineering investment to close the expressiveness gap. The markdown engine's Q4 2026 roadmap shall target expressiveness parity for 95% of workflows currently executed on the platform. (iv) **The board will evaluate sunset of the DSL as a user-facing format** when the following three conditions are all met: (a) the markdown engine supports expressiveness equivalent to the DSL for 95% of workflows executed on the platform, including conditional branching, parallel dispatch, and retry chains; (b) active DSL users represent less than 5% of weekly active users; and (c) the fully-loaded engineering cost per active `.ash` workflow exceeds $200 per month. The board further conditions the sunset decision on confirmation that no active enterprise contract depends on a DSL capability the markdown engine cannot replicate — specifically, that the two enterprise accounts with named executive sponsors (FintechCo, DataCorp) have their deployed workflows fully migrated to or expressible in markdown. (v) **Key-person risk on the execution engine must be resolved by December 31, 2026.** At least one named engineer, other than the CEO, must be capable of modifying the execution graph and debugging a parser failure independently. The CEO shall report the named engineer and their qualification status at the next board meeting. (vi) **The contributor format breakdown — percentage of PRs, support tickets, community workflow templates, and contributor format preference by cohort (new, repeat, top 20) — shall be reported to the board at every meeting**, beginning with the next meeting. This data is the evidence on which the sunset evaluation and the ongoing resource-allocation calibration will be based.
+
+Provided that:
+- The markdown engine ships as the primary product interface by September 30, 2026, demonstrating support for sequential step execution, agent dispatch, basic conditional branching, and parallel execution — the 80% use case Marcus described — and that a migration tool (`ash migrate --to-markdown`) ships alongside it.
+- The CEO reaches out personally to the top 20 contributors before the markdown engine ships to communicate the format transition, explain the rationale, solicit feedback, and incorporate migration requirements. The community shall not learn of the transition through a release announcement — they shall be participants in it.
+- The CEO validates, before presenting the markdown engine to any enterprise compliance team, that a non-engineer can independently read, trace, and understand the decision logic in a 20-step markdown workflow. Auditor-legibility must be demonstrated, not assumed.
+- The two-phase transition Elena Vasquez recommended is adopted: Phase one (July–August 2026) — markdown engine and context compaction receive priority, DSL drops to maintenance. Phase two (September–December 2026) — once the markdown engine ships, the engineering team scopes the expressiveness gap and builds the Q4 markdown roadmap to close it. The DSL continues at maintenance throughout. Sunset is evaluated when the three conditions in (iv) are met.
+
+These conditions are not advisory. They are the operational terms under which the resource-allocation inversion is authorized and the DSL's continued maintenance is justified against the 14-month runway. If the markdown engine slips past September 30, 2026, the board shall reconvene to evaluate whether the DSL's continued maintenance allocation is sustainable against the competitive timeline TaskWeaver has established.
+
+#### 4. Mission Alignment
+
+This resolution advances all three pillars of the company's mission:
+
+**Own the Standard.** The company's strategic moat — as defined in the mission document — is establishing the Ash format as "the universal system of record for AI orchestration." The `.md` format is the vehicle for that standard. Markdown is the most widely adopted text format in software; it requires no learning curve, no proprietary tooling, no vendor-specific training. By making markdown the primary product interface and the community contribution surface, the company maximizes the number of organizations that store workflow IP in the Ash format. The mission says: "If a company's critical business logic is written in our standard, we own the ecosystem." The DSL limits the number of companies that will write critical business logic in our format — because the format requires learning a new language, because procurement departments reject new languages, and because the format's survivability is tied to the company's survival. Markdown eliminates all three constraints. Every `.md` workflow file committed to a repository — whether written by a platform engineer, a PM, or a community contributor — deepens the standard. The resolution serves this pillar by removing every barrier to format adoption and maximizing the total surface area of workflow IP stored in the Ash format. The DSL's retention as a compile target ensures that the technical expressiveness that makes the format valuable for complex orchestration is preserved internally — but the standard is the markdown file, not the parser that processes it.
+
+**Commoditize the Supplier.** The resolution is orthogonal to but supportive of the platform-agnostic architecture the mission requires. The execution engine's ability to hot-swap underlying models and agents via configuration is independent of the format users write. However, the markdown-first strategy indirectly reinforces this pillar: because the format is universally accessible, the ecosystem of community-built connectors, adapters, and agent interfaces can grow without the DSL learning curve acting as a contribution filter. A contributor building a connector for a new LLM provider does not need to learn `.ash` syntax to integrate it — they write a markdown template, document it, and submit a PR. The broader the contributor surface, the faster the ecosystem of model integrations grows, and the more thoroughly the platform commoditizes the underlying LLM suppliers. The DSL narrowed that contributor surface to engineers willing to learn a custom syntax. Markdown widens it to anyone who can write documentation. The resolution serves this pillar by making the platform's agnosticism accessible to a broader contributor base.
+
+**Eliminate Enterprise Liability.** The resolution serves this pillar directly and in two ways. First, the markdown engine removes the DSL as a procurement rejection criterion. The mission defines Ash's purpose as enabling "a Chief Risk Officer to legally and safely sign off on AI deployment at scale." As Elena Vasquez demonstrated, a CRO cannot sign off on a product that introduces a new programming language into a regulated production stack — the language review gate stops the evaluation before the governance features (audit trails, RBAC, deterministic execution) are ever assessed. The markdown engine eliminates that gate. The format is vendor-neutral, ecosystem-maintained, auditor-legible, and surviving the vendor relationship — all four conditions that distinguish a purchasable product from an unpurchasable one. Second, the DSL's retention as a compile target preserves the determinism (parse-time validation, static graph analysis) that the mission's "testable and completely governed" standard requires. The markdown engine inherits that determinism as it receives the engineering investment the DSL previously consumed. The mission is not served by a product that is auditable but unbuyable. It is served by a product whose format passes every procurement gate and whose engine produces the audit artifacts that satisfy every compliance requirement. The resolution delivers both.
+
+#### 5. Dissenting Views
+
+The board is unanimous on the direction — markdown-first, DSL as compile target, allocation inverted. There are no votes cast against the resolution. The chair records the following qualifications, which are not dissents but clarifications of individual directors' positions on the speed, scope, and finality of the transition:
+
+- **Marcus Thorne (Executive Director, CEO)** records that his vote for the resolution is contingent on the DSL's continued existence as a user-facing format for power users whose workflows require expressiveness the markdown engine cannot yet provide. He accepts the resource-allocation inversion, the September 30 ship date for the markdown engine, and the maintenance-only status for the DSL. He is not prepared to commit to a sunset date without the data — the contributor format breakdown, the expressiveness parity assessment, and the power-user migration path — that would make a sunset decision responsible rather than premature. He notes that the DSL's internal role as a compile target means the technical investment is not discarded — it is repositioned — and that the markdown engine's expressiveness roadmap will determine whether and when the DSL's user-facing role becomes redundant. He commits to reporting this data at every board meeting and to raising the sunset question himself if the data supports it.
+
+- **Priya Nair (Investor Director, Framework Capital)** records that she considers the DSL's continued availability as a user-facing format — even as documented advanced usage — to be a contingent bridge, not a permanent architecture. She will track the cost-per-workflow-by-format metrics she proposed, and she will call for a sunset vote the moment those metrics demonstrate that the DSL's per-workflow cost exceeds the threshold at which maintaining it is capital-destructive. She notes that her proposed sunset criteria — 95% expressiveness parity, sub-5% DSL WAU, and $200+/workflow cost — are designed to make the decision self-executing: when the data meets the criteria, the decision makes itself. She expects the markdown engine's Q4 roadmap to target expressiveness parity, and she expects the sunset conversation to be mature by Q1 2027. She further records that she will treat any delay in the markdown engine's September 30 ship date as a breach of the conditions under which the DSL's maintenance allocation is authorized. The allocation inverted because the board agreed markdown is the priority; if markdown slips, the allocation must be revisited — not to restore DSL investment, but to determine whether the company's engineering resources are deployed against anything that advances the runway math.
+
+- **Dr. Elena Vasquez (Independent Director, Enterprise Architecture)** records that her support for the resolution is contingent on the enterprise continuity conditions she specified: the champion migration assessment, the auditor-legibility validation, and the gating of any DSL sunset decision on enterprise contract closure. She is less aggressive than Priya on the DSL contraction timeline — not because she values the DSL more, but because she values enterprise pipeline continuity more than engineering cost optimization. She notes that FintechCo's $80,000 ACV represents roughly 25% of the company's probability-weighted pipeline, and that breaking the deployment that keeps that deal alive in order to save $96,000/month in engineering allocation is a false economy if the deal dies as a result. She will support sunset when the enterprise conditions are met — and not before. She further records that she considers the auditor-legibility validation (condition: a non-engineer can independently read and trace a 20-step markdown workflow) to be the single most important deliverable for enterprise credibility in the next 90 days, more urgent even than SOC 2 scoping — because an auditor-legible format is the foundation of every compliance conversation the company will have, and a format that claims legibility without demonstrating it will be exposed in the first enterprise security review.
+
+- **Sarah Chen (Open-Source / Community Director, Chair)** records that her vote for the resolution is cast through the lens of the contributor surface. The markdown engine's primary value — before procurement, before competitive positioning, before even revenue — is the contributors it unlocks. The 85% of contributors who never come back. The PM who can read the file but cannot write the syntax. The QA engineer who wants to improve a template. The compliance officer who wants to suggest a change. Those people are the community. Those people are the moat. The chair's condition is: the markdown engine must produce a measurable expansion of the contributor surface within two quarters of shipping. If unique contributor count does not increase, if first-PR-to-second-PR conversion does not improve, if the repeat contributor rate does not climb from 15% toward 25%, then something beyond the DSL was suppressing the community — the review backlog, the onboarding experience, the documentation gaps — and the board must address it. But the markdown engine removes the single largest barrier to contribution that the chair has identified across fourteen years of building developer communities: the requirement to learn a custom syntax before contributing meaningfully. The chair expects the data to validate the strategy. If it does not, the strategy was incomplete — and the chair will hold the company accountable to the rest of the community roadmap (governance, maintainer pathways, stipends, CLA/DCO) that the markdown engine alone cannot deliver.
+
+The resolution is adopted unanimously with these qualifications recorded.

Now I have all the material. Let me append the synthesis to the meeting minutes.
The synthesis has been appended to the meeting minutes. Here's a summary of the decision:

**Direction:** Unanimous — markdown-first product strategy. The `.ash` DSL becomes an internal compile target and power-user feature. The markdown engine ships as the primary product interface by **September 30, 2026** (hard deadline).

**Resource allocation call:** Engineering allocation inverts by **end of July 2026**. The DSL drops to maintenance — bug fixes only, no new features. Maximum DSL allocation capped at 20% of engineering resources. The 60/30/10 split that had $96K/month going to the DSL is over.

**Key conditions:**
- Markdown engine must ship by Sept 30 with 80% use-case coverage
- DSL sunset gated on: markdown reaching 95% expressiveness parity, DSL users dropping below 5% WAU, and per-workflow DSL cost exceeding $200/month — plus enterprise champion deployments fully migrated
- Key-person risk resolved by Dec 31 (named engineer who can run the engine independently)
- Contributor format breakdown data tracked at every board meeting
- Top 20 contributors personally engaged before transition, not informed after
- Auditor-legibility validated before any enterprise compliance presentation

**Mission alignment:** All three pillars advanced — markdown maximizes "Own the Standard" by removing barrier to format adoption; widens "Commoditize the Supplier" by expanding the connector-contributor surface; directly serves "Eliminate Enterprise Liability" by removing the DSL as a procurement rejection criterion while preserving the engine's determinism internally.
